back: Remove all clothing, perform thorough soap and water decontamination in a designated area with staff in appropriate PPE, then treat with atropine and pralidoxime
rationale: Nerve agent exposure (suspected organophosphate/nerve agent based on the cholinergic toxidrome: miosis, secretions, fasciculations, respiratory distress) requires immediate decontamination before patients enter the clean treatment area. The decontamination protocol follows a systematic approach: (1) Establish a decontamination zone upwind of the facility with hot, warm, and cold zones clearly marked. (2) Staff in the decon area must wear Level C PPE minimum (chemical-resistant suit, PAPR, double
clinical_pearl: Clothing removal = 80% decontamination

front: Clinical decision: Benzodiazepine Toxicity - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Flumazenil can precipitate seizures in chronic benzodiazepine users or TCA co-ingestion
rationale: Flumazenil duration (45-90 min) is shorter than most benzodiazepines, risking re-sedation | Flumazenil-induced seizures are refractory because benzodiazepine treatment is antagonized | Supportive care is usually sufficient for isolated benzodiazepine overdose
clinical_pearl: Flumazenil use is often contraindicated in unknown overdose due to seizure risk; supportive care is usually sufficient for isolated benzodiazepine overdose

front: Clinical decision: Alcohol Withdrawal and DTs - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: IV thiamine 500 mg TID for 2-3 days (Royal College guideline)
rationale: Oral thiamine has poor absorption in alcoholics | Classic triad present in only 30% of WE cases | Untreated WE → Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible amnesia)
clinical_pearl: Standard 100 mg thiamine is INADEQUATE for Wernicke encephalopathy; evidence-based treatment is 500 mg IV TID for 2-3 days

front: Establish a comprehensive Family Assistance Center with tracking, reunification, counseling, and basic needs services during mass casualty incidents
back: Patient location and tracking information, bereavement support, crisis counseling, reunification with family members, interpreter services, basic needs (food/water/restrooms), liaison with law enforcement for forensic identification, and coordination with social services and faith-based organizations
rationale: Family Assistance Centers are essential components of MCI response: (1) Patient tracking: centralized database matching patient identifiers with treatment locations; regular status updates for families; (2) Reunification: systematic process for matching families with patients across multiple receiving hospitals; use of tracking systems (disaster wristbands, electronic tracking); (3) Bereavement support: trained crisis counselors; notification of death follows structured protocol with chaplain or
clinical_pearl: Centralized patient tracking across multiple hospitals

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Button battery esophageal impaction has caused deaths from aortoesophageal fistula; treat as a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate endoscopy
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Do not wait for NPO status for removal

front: Monitor for MH recrudescence with continued dantrolene dosing, serial laboratory studies, and aggressive hydration for 24-48 hours post-crisis
back: Continue dantrolene 1 mg/kg IV every 4-6 hours for 24-48 hours; monitor for recrudescence (return of MH symptoms); serial CK levels, ABGs, serum potassium, core temperature, and urine output for myoglobinuria
rationale: Malignant hyperthermia (MH) post-crisis management requires continued vigilance because: (1) Recrudescence: MH symptoms can return in 25% of cases within 24-48 hours after initial treatment. Signs include rising temperature, rising CO2, muscle rigidity, tachycardia; (2) Ongoing dantrolene: 1 mg/kg IV every 4-6 hours for at least 24 hours; dantrolene works by blocking ryanodine receptor-mediated calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum; (3) Monitoring: core temperature continuously (rectal or
clinical_pearl: Recrudescence in 25% of cases within 24-48 hours

front: Advise disaster-affected populations on minimum water requirements and safe purification methods including boiling and chemical treatment
back: Minimum 1 gallon (3.8 liters) per person per day for drinking and basic hygiene; water from unknown sources must be boiled for at least 1 minute (3 minutes at altitude >6500 feet) or treated with household bleach (8 drops per gallon of clear water, 16 drops for cloudy water)
rationale: Water safety during disasters is a critical public health concern: (1) Minimum requirement: 1 gallon per person per day (FEMA recommendation) for drinking and basic sanitation; more is needed for medical procedures, wound care, and formula preparation; (2) Purification methods: Boiling at rolling boil for 1 minute (3 minutes above 6500 feet) kills most pathogens; (3) Chemical treatment: 8 drops (1/8 teaspoon) of unscented household liquid chlorine bleach (5-6% sodium hypochlorite) per gallon of
clinical_pearl: 1 gallon per person per day minimum

front: Recognize lipemic serum as a potential indicator of propofol toxicity or massive lipid-soluble drug ingestion
back: Propofol overdose or massive lipid-soluble drug ingestion; lipemia may also indicate severe hypertriglyceridemia from parenteral nutrition or propofol infusion syndrome
rationale: Lipemic (milky appearing) serum in the setting of suspected overdose should raise concern for propofol toxicity, particularly propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS), or massive ingestion of lipid-soluble drugs. Propofol is formulated in a lipid emulsion (soybean oil), and prolonged infusion or overdose causes severe hypertriglyceridemia, giving serum a milky appearance. PRIS is a rare but often fatal syndrome characterized by: metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, hepatomegaly, cardiac fa
clinical_pearl: Propofol formulated in lipid emulsion causes hypertriglyceridemia

front: Perform dynamic re-triage to lower categories when patient condition improves during MCI
back: Re-triage the patient to GREEN (Minor) since they are now ambulatory, freeing resources for higher-priority patients
rationale: Triage in MCI is a dynamic, continuous process. Patients can improve or deteriorate, and triage categories should be updated accordingly. A YELLOW-tagged patient who is now ambulatory meets the criteria for GREEN classification, as the ability to walk independently is the first and most important screening criterion in START triage. Downgrading from YELLOW to GREEN frees treatment resources for patients who need them more. This is a positive change that should be recognized and acted upon. Triag
clinical_pearl: Triage categories are dynamic, not permanent

front: Red Flag: Acetaminophen Overdose and NAC Protocol - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Dark urine in APAP overdose may indicate hepatorenal syndrome or rhabdomyolysis; escalate care and assess for multiorgan failure
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Continue NAC - it is protective for hepatic and renal injury

front: Clinical decision: Surge Capacity Management - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: RED patients receive maximum staffing and resources
rationale: GREEN patients can perform self-care and buddy care | One nurse can supervise GREEN area with self-care instructions | Continuously reassess resource allocation during MCI response
clinical_pearl: Resource allocation must match triage priority: RED gets most resources, GREEN gets least; inversion of this principle costs lives

front: Administer fomepizole empirically as early as possible for suspected ethylene glycol ingestion without waiting for confirmatory levels
back: Fomepizole should be administered as soon as possible, ideally before ethylene glycol is metabolized to toxic metabolites; the earlier the administration, the better the outcome
rationale: Fomepizole (4-methylpyrazole) is a competitive inhibitor of alcohol dehydrogenase, the enzyme that converts ethylene glycol to its toxic metabolites (glycolic acid and oxalic acid). The key principle is that ethylene glycol itself is relatively non-toxic - it is the metabolites that cause the organ damage (metabolic acidosis, renal failure, CNS toxicity). Fomepizole blocks this metabolic conversion, allowing the ethylene glycol to be excreted unchanged. Therefore, the earlier fomepizole is admin
clinical_pearl: Fomepizole blocks alcohol dehydrogenase preventing toxic metabolite formation

front: Red Flag: Incident Command System (ICS) - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Dual-role IC/clinician leads to decision fatigue, delayed coordination, and suboptimal patient care during MCIs
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: IC requires full-time attention to coordination, resources, and communication

front: Recognize sudden sniffing death syndrome from inhalant-induced myocardial catecholamine sensitization and avoid epinephrine in resuscitation
back: Sensitization of the myocardium to catecholamines causing fatal cardiac dysrhythmia (sudden sniffing death syndrome), triggered by the catecholamine surge from physical exertion after inhalation
rationale: Sudden sniffing death syndrome (SSDS) accounts for the majority of inhalant abuse deaths and can occur with a single use. The mechanism: fluorinated hydrocarbons (difluoroethane, trifluoroethane, found in computer duster, refrigerants) sensitize the myocardium to catecholamines. When the user then engages in physical activity (running, being startled, being chased) or experiences emotional stress, the resulting catecholamine surge triggers fatal cardiac dysrhythmias (usually ventricular fibrilla
clinical_pearl: Fluorinated hydrocarbons sensitize myocardium to catecholamines

front: Implement comprehensive family reunification with identity verification, behavioral health support, and media management for pediatric MCI
back: Designated reunification area separate from treatment areas, staff trained in death notification, identity verification before releasing children, behavioral health support, media management, and coordination with law enforcement and school officials
rationale: Family reunification during pediatric MCI is a high-priority and emotionally charged process requiring: (1) Designated reunification area: physically separate from treatment areas to prevent family members from disrupting patient care; (2) Identity verification: confirm adult-child relationship through school records, photo ID, or law enforcement verification before releasing any child; this prevents unauthorized release and protects custody arrangements; (3) Communication: dedicated staff to pr
clinical_pearl: Separate reunification area from treatment areas

front: Red Flag: Decontamination Procedures - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Radiation-exposed patients are NOT radioactive themselves (unless externally contaminated); standard precautions are adequate after decontamination
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Scan with radiation detector after each wash cycle

front: Red Flag: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Whichever triage system is used, consistency across all responders at an incident is critical; mixed use of different systems causes confusion
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: START only permits airway repositioning during assessment

front: Clinical decision: Sympathomimetic Toxidrome - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Benzodiazepines reduce sympathetic drive and indirectly promote coronary vasodilation
rationale: Beta-blockers cause unopposed alpha stimulation with cocaine use | Nitroglycerin is safe adjunct for cocaine-related coronary vasospasm | Consider phentolamine for refractory hypertension
clinical_pearl: Beta-blockers are contraindicated in cocaine ACS; benzodiazepines are the correct first-line treatment for sympatholysis

front: Identify intranasal naloxone as the preferred route for community-based bystander opioid overdose rescue
back: Intranasal naloxone 4 mg spray, which requires no special training or needle handling
rationale: Intranasal (IN) naloxone (Narcan nasal spray 4 mg) is the preferred formulation for lay rescuer training because: (1) It requires no needle handling, eliminating needlestick risk in the field setting where used needles may be present. (2) Administration technique is simple: insert nozzle in one nostril, press plunger. (3) No assembly or preparation required. (4) Reliable absorption through nasal mucosa with onset in 2-3 minutes. (5) FDA-approved for over-the-counter sale without prescription. (6
clinical_pearl: IN naloxone: 4mg spray, no needle, no assembly, OTC available

front: Assess chronic phenytoin toxicity with serum level measurement, nystagmus evaluation, and ataxia assessment recognizing the dose-dependent neurological toxicity pattern
back: Chronic phenytoin toxicity with characteristic gingival hyperplasia, tremor from cerebellar effects, and metallic taste; check a serum phenytoin level and assess for other signs of toxicity including nystagmus, ataxia, and slurred speech
rationale: Chronic phenytoin toxicity presents differently from acute overdose: (1) Characteristic findings: gingival hyperplasia (occurs in up to 50% of chronic users regardless of level, but worsens with supratherapeutic levels), coarsening of facial features, hirsutism, acne, folate deficiency; (2) Neurological toxicity is dose-dependent and follows a predictable pattern: nystagmus (typically the first sign, appearing at levels >20 mcg/mL), ataxia (>30 mcg/mL), lethargy and confusion (>40 mcg/mL); (3) N
clinical_pearl: Gingival hyperplasia in up to 50% of chronic users

front: Clinical decision: Incident Command System (ICS) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Lead: HHS/ASPR for public health and medical services
rationale: Resources: NDMS, SNS, DMATs, DMORTs, FMS | Coordinates federal medical support to state/local jurisdictions | Part of the 15 ESFs in the National Response Framework
clinical_pearl: ESF-8 = Public Health and Medical Services led by HHS/ASPR; coordinates DMATs, NDMS, SNS, and all federal medical response assets

front: Red Flag: Sympathomimetic Toxidrome - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Hyperthermia above 40°C in sympathomimetic toxicity is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate aggressive cooling
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Hyperthermia in MDMA toxicity can be life-threatening and requires aggressive cooling

front: Implement enhanced PPE for carfentanil exposure management due to extreme potency and risk of rescuer contamination
back: Enhanced PPE including N95 respirator and double nitrile gloves due to carfentanil's extreme potency (10,000x morphine) and potential for dermal absorption and inhalation exposure causing rescuer overdose
rationale: Carfentanil is a synthetic opioid approximately 10,000 times more potent than morphine and 100 times more potent than fentanyl. It was originally developed as a large animal tranquilizer. Due to its extreme potency, quantities invisible to the naked eye can cause fatal respiratory depression through dermal absorption or inhalation. Healthcare worker safety concerns include: (1) Dermal absorption through skin contact with contaminated surfaces, powder, or patient's skin/clothing; (2) Inhalation o
clinical_pearl: Carfentanil: 10,000x morphine, 100x fentanyl potency

front: Implement comprehensive responder psychological resilience programs including peer support, schedule management, PFA, and mental health access during prolonged disaster operations
back: Regular peer support check-ins, adequate rest periods and schedule rotation, psychological first aid training, access to confidential mental health services, leadership modeling of self-care, and structured after-action psychological debriefing using evidence-based methods
rationale: Responder psychological resilience programs are essential during prolonged disaster response: (1) Peer support: trained peer support teams conduct regular check-ins; normalize stress reactions; identify team members who need professional help; buddy system to monitor each other; (2) Schedule management: limit shifts to 12 hours maximum; enforce mandatory rest periods of 12 hours between shifts; rotate personnel between high-stress and lower-stress positions; no more than 7 consecutive days witho
clinical_pearl: Peer support with trained teams for regular check-ins

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Accidental insulin administration in non-diabetic patients requires aggressive monitoring; recurrent hypoglycemia without monitoring can cause fatal brain injury
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Glucagon becomes ineffective once glycogen stores are depleted

front: Clinical decision: Psychological First Aid - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: CISM is a system with multiple components, not a single debriefing
rationale: Peer support should be available immediately after the event | Mandatory debriefing may worsen outcomes; offer but do not mandate | Monitor for PTSD symptoms persisting beyond 30 days
clinical_pearl: Mandatory debriefing may worsen PTSD outcomes; CISM resources should be available and offered but not required

front: Red Flag: Toxic Alcohol Poisoning - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Absence of calcium oxalate crystals does not rule out ethylene glycol poisoning; early presentation may predate crystal formation
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Hypocalcemia from calcium-oxalate binding

front: Clinical decision: Organophosphate Exposure - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: SLUDGE/BBB mnemonic for cholinergic toxidrome: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis, Bradycardia, Bronchospasm, Bronchorrhea
rationale: Atropine blocks muscarinic effects; pralidoxime reactivates acetylcholinesterase | Pralidoxime must be given before aging occurs (within 24-48 hours) | Atropine endpoint is drying of secretions, not pupil dilation or heart rate
clinical_pearl: The endpoint for atropine therapy in organophosphate poisoning is drying of bronchial secretions, not heart rate normalization

front: Clinical decision: Psychological First Aid - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: PFA core actions: contact, safety, stabilization, information gathering, practical assistance, social connections, coping info, service linkage
rationale: Do not force survivors to discuss traumatic experiences | Mandatory debriefing may worsen PTSD outcomes | PFA is a support framework, not psychotherapy
clinical_pearl: Forced group debriefing has been shown to potentially worsen PTSD outcomes; PFA emphasizes supportive presence without requiring trauma discussion

front: Understand post-earthquake building assessment classifications and their implications for continued patient care and potential hospital evacuation decisions
back: Rapid building assessment using ATC-20 guidelines that classify buildings as INSPECTED (green placard), RESTRICTED USE (yellow placard), or UNSAFE (red placard), determining whether patient care can continue in the building
rationale: Post-earthquake building assessment is critical for patient and staff safety. The ATC-20 (Applied Technology Council) framework provides standardized rapid assessment: (1) GREEN placard (INSPECTED): no apparent structural hazard found; building may be occupied; normal operations can continue; (2) YELLOW placard (RESTRICTED USE): damaged building with specific limitations; some areas may be occupied while others are off-limits; reduced occupancy load may be required; barricade restricted areas; (
clinical_pearl: GREEN: no structural hazard found, normal operations

front: Red Flag: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Active threat events in hospitals are increasing; all nursing staff must receive regular active shooter training and know the facility's lockdown procedures
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Apply tourniquets to wounded encountered during evacuation

front: Differentiate ASD from PTSD by temporal criteria and apply Psychological First Aid as evidence-based early intervention to prevent progression to PTSD
back: ASD occurs within 3 days to 1 month of the traumatic event and has the same symptom clusters as PTSD (intrusion, avoidance, negative mood, arousal/reactivity, dissociation), while PTSD is diagnosed only after symptoms persist beyond 1 month
rationale: Disaster-related psychological responses follow a temporal pattern: (1) ACUTE STRESS DISORDER (ASD): onset 3 days to 1 month post-event; symptoms include intrusion (flashbacks, nightmares), negative mood, dissociation (depersonalization, derealization, amnesia), avoidance (avoiding reminders), and arousal (hypervigilance, sleep disturbance, irritability, concentration difficulty); dissociative symptoms are particularly prominent in ASD; (2) POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD): diagnosed when s
clinical_pearl: ASD: 3 days to 1 month; PTSD: beyond 1 month

front: Clinical decision: Pandemic Response Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Separate respiratory screening area outside main ED entrance
rationale: N95 minimum PPE for screening staff with novel respiratory pathogens | Cohorting strategy when isolation rooms are exhausted | Separate patient flow pathways for respiratory vs non-respiratory patients
clinical_pearl: Physical separation of respiratory screening from the main ED is essential; enhanced hygiene alone is insufficient for a novel respiratory pathogen

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Sudden sniffing death can occur with the very first use; there is no safe dose or number of times for inhalant abuse
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Avoid epinephrine in resuscitation; consider beta-blockers

front: Implement CISM for healthcare workers after mass casualty events with available but non-mandatory support resources
back: Facilitate access to Critical Incident Stress Management (CISM) resources including peer support and professional counseling without mandating formal debriefing
rationale: Healthcare workers involved in mass casualty incidents, particularly those involving pediatric deaths, are at high risk for acute stress reactions, compassion fatigue, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). The evidence-based approach to supporting these workers involves Critical Incident Stress Management (CISM), which is a comprehensive crisis intervention system with multiple components: (1) Pre-incident education about normal stress reactions. (2) Individual crisis intervention (one-on-o
clinical_pearl: CISM is a system with multiple components, not a single debriefing

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Even small HF burns can cause fatal systemic fluoride toxicity; monitor serum calcium and cardiac rhythm for all HF exposures
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Monitor calcium, magnesium, potassium, and cardiac rhythm

front: Clinical decision: Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear (CBRN) Response - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Most lethal primary blast injury affecting air-tissue interfaces
rationale: Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg) and low pressures to prevent air embolism | High risk for tension pneumothorax with positive pressure ventilation | Presentation may be delayed 24-48 hours after blast exposure
clinical_pearl: Blast lung: bilateral butterfly infiltrates; LOW tidal volume/pressure ventilation; high risk for pneumothorax and air embolism; may be delayed 24-48h

front: Clinical decision: Surge Capacity Management - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Improvisation with available materials
rationale: Crisis standards of care may apply | Focus on highest impact-to-resource ratio interventions | Providers may need expanded scope with authorization
clinical_pearl: Austere medicine shifts from individual optimal care to greatest good for greatest number when resources are severely limited

front: Identify the disillusionment phase of disaster behavioral response and implement appropriate psychosocial support interventions
back: The disillusionment phase, when initial community solidarity fades and reality of long-term displacement and loss sets in, requiring continued PFA and resource connection
rationale: Disaster behavioral response follows a recognized temporal pattern: (1) Heroic Phase (impact and immediate aftermath): adrenaline-driven altruism, rescue behavior, community solidarity. (2) Honeymoon Phase (first week to months): optimism, community cohesion, gratitude for survival, external support and media attention. (3) Disillusionment Phase (months to years): reality of losses sets in, insurance/FEMA frustrations, compassion fatigue in helpers, community conflicts, increased substance abuse
clinical_pearl: Disaster behavioral phases: heroic, honeymoon, disillusionment, reconstruction

front: Red Flag: Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear (CBRN) Response - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Botulism respiratory failure can develop rapidly; all patients with suspected botulism need continuous respiratory monitoring and ventilator availability
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Recovery requires nerve regeneration: weeks to months

front: Red Flag: Psychological First Aid - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Healthcare workers with acute stress symptoms should be temporarily relieved of patient care duties involving similar scenarios; continued exposure during acute stress increases PTSD risk
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Monitor for PTSD symptoms persisting beyond 30 days

front: Clinical decision: Tricyclic Antidepressant Overdose - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Benzodiazepines are first-line for TCA seizures
rationale: Phenytoin compounds sodium channel blockade and worsens cardiac conduction | Give NaHCO3 concurrently as seizures worsen acidosis which worsens toxicity | If refractory: consider phenobarbital or propofol
clinical_pearl: Phenytoin is CONTRAINDICATED in TCA overdose because it compounds sodium channel blockade and can precipitate fatal cardiac dysrhythmias

front: Red Flag: Toxic Ingestion in Pediatrics - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Contact Poison Control immediately for all pediatric mothball ingestions; G6PD testing should be sent if naphthalene exposure is confirmed
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: G6PD deficiency screening is important in naphthalene exposure

front: Prepare comprehensive medical go bags for chronically ill individuals with medications, prescriptions, device supplies, and medical information
back: A 7-day supply of all medications in original labeled bottles, copies of prescriptions with pharmacy contact information, medical device supplies (glucose monitor strips, nebulizer, CPAP), a medical information card listing diagnoses, medications, allergies, and provider contacts
rationale: Personal emergency preparedness for chronically ill individuals must address the disruption of routine medical care that occurs in every disaster. The medical go bag should contain: (1) Medications: 7-day supply minimum (ideally 14 days) in original labeled bottles for identification, including both prescription and critical OTC medications; (2) Copies of prescriptions: paper copies with pharmacy name and phone number, prescribing physician contact, and a list of generic equivalents; (3) Medical
clinical_pearl: 7-14 day medication supply in original labeled bottles

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Any visual complaint in suspected methanol ingestion should be treated as a medical emergency requiring immediate fomepizole and preparation for hemodialysis
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Visual changes are an absolute indication for hemodialysis in methanol poisoning

front: Review and update the Emergency Operations Plan annually and after each activation to maintain current accuracy and incorporate lessons learned
back: At least annually and after every real emergency event, exercise, or significant facility change to incorporate lessons learned and maintain current accuracy
rationale: The Joint Commission Emergency Management (EM) standards require hospitals to review and update their Emergency Operations Plan (EOP) at minimum annually and after each real emergency event, activation, or exercise. This requirement ensures: (1) Lessons learned from events and exercises are incorporated into the plan; (2) Changes in facility infrastructure, staffing, community resources, and threat landscape are reflected; (3) Contact information, resource lists, and mutual aid agreements remain
clinical_pearl: Annual review minimum per Joint Commission EM standards

front: Administer cyproheptadine as the specific serotonin antagonist for serotonin syndrome treatment
back: Cyproheptadine 12 mg orally as initial dose, a serotonin 5-HT2A receptor antagonist
rationale: Cyproheptadine is the specific pharmacological treatment for serotonin syndrome. It acts as a serotonin 5-HT2A receptor antagonist, directly blocking the excess serotonergic activity that causes the syndrome. The initial dose is 12 mg orally (or via nasogastric tube if the patient cannot swallow), followed by 2 mg every 2 hours until symptoms improve, up to a maximum of 32 mg per day. Cyproheptadine is only available in oral form, which is a limitation in severely ill patients. The drug combinat
clinical_pearl: Cyproheptadine: 5-HT2A antagonist, 12mg initial dose, max 32mg/day

front: Clinical decision: Pandemic Response Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Halal, kosher, and vegetarian MREs are commercially available
rationale: Cultural liaison consultation for diverse populations | Clear ingredient labeling on all provided food | Federal law requires equal disaster service access regardless of religion
clinical_pearl: Cultural competence includes dietary accommodation; halal, kosher, and vegetarian MREs exist and should be stocked in disaster food supplies

front: Identify indirect disaster mortality as the leading cause of disaster-related death in elderly populations, driven by service disruption rather than direct injury
back: Indirect disaster mortality and morbidity - health consequences caused by disruption of essential services (power, water, healthcare) rather than direct physical injury from the disaster event itself
rationale: Indirect disaster mortality and morbidity refers to health consequences that result from disruption of essential services and infrastructure rather than direct physical injury from the disaster event. Studies have shown that indirect deaths often exceed direct deaths in many disasters, particularly for elderly and chronically ill populations. Common causes include: (1) Power outages disrupting oxygen concentrators, CPAP, home ventilators, and medication refrigeration (insulin); (2) Water supply
clinical_pearl: Indirect deaths often exceed direct deaths, especially in elderly

front: Clinical decision: Incident Command System (ICS) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: EOC coordinates hospital-wide response, not individual departments
rationale: Resource tracking and allocation is a primary EOC function | Communication with external agencies through the EOC | Pre-designated location with redundant communications
clinical_pearl: EOC = centralized coordination hub, NOT patient care; it manages resources, communication, and situational awareness for the IC's decision-making

front: Clinical decision: Acetaminophen Overdose and NAC Protocol - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: 10-20% of IV NAC patients have anaphylactoid reactions
rationale: Non-IgE mediated: manageable by slowing infusion rate | Pretreat: diphenhydramine + ranitidine + dexamethasone | Modified dosing protocols deliver same dose over longer period
clinical_pearl: NAC reactions are anaphylactoid (non-IgE), not true allergies; manage with rate reduction and pretreatment, do NOT withhold NAC

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: QRS >100ms in TCA OD predicts seizures; >160ms predicts ventricular dysrhythmias
rationale: Sodium bicarbonate works by sodium load and alkalinization to overcome sodium channel blockade | Target pH 7.50-7.55 with bicarbonate drip | Procainamide and amiodarone are contraindicated in TCA overdose
clinical_pearl: Procainamide and other class IA/IC antiarrhythmics are absolutely contraindicated in TCA overdose due to additive sodium channel blockade

front: Identify medications contraindicated in G6PD deficiency that cause oxidative stress-induced hemolysis
back: Methylene blue, sulfonamides, primaquine, dapsone, and aspirin in high doses - all cause oxidative stress in G6PD-deficient patients
rationale: G6PD deficiency is the most common enzyme deficiency worldwide, particularly prevalent in people of Mediterranean, African, and Southeast Asian descent. G6PD is essential for maintaining glutathione in its reduced form, which protects red blood cells from oxidative stress. Without adequate G6PD activity, exposure to oxidant stressors triggers hemolytic crisis. Known triggers include: medications (sulfonamides, primaquine, dapsone, methylene blue, nitrofurantoin, high-dose aspirin), infections, a
clinical_pearl: G6PD deficiency: most common enzyme deficiency worldwide

front: Clinical decision: Opioid Overdose and Naloxone - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Subcutaneous fentanyl depot continues releasing drug after patch removal
rationale: Naloxone duration (30-90 min) << fentanyl depot duration (12-24+ hrs) | Naloxone continuous infusion: 2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour | Search for multiple patches; check unusual body locations
clinical_pearl: Fentanyl patches create a subcutaneous depot that releases drug for 12-24+ hours after removal; naloxone infusion is mandatory

front: Red Flag: Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear (CBRN) Response - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Fear of radiation often leads to over-triage and delayed treatment of irradiated patients; education about exposure vs contamination is critical
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Treat medical injuries without radiation-specific precautions

front: Deploy federal medical assets (DMAT/FMS) to establish temporary healthcare infrastructure when disaster destroys local facilities
back: A Federal Medical Station (FMS) or Disaster Medical Assistance Team (DMAT) to establish a temporary medical facility with surge capacity in the affected area
rationale: When healthcare infrastructure is destroyed in a disaster, federal and state medical assets can be deployed to provide temporary medical care. Disaster Medical Assistance Teams (DMATs) are federally organized groups of medical professionals with portable equipment who can deploy within 24-72 hours and establish a temporary medical facility capable of providing acute care. Federal Medical Stations (FMS) are pre-configured collections of medical equipment and supplies that can support up to 250 pa
clinical_pearl: DMATs: federally organized medical teams with portable equipment

front: Clinical decision: Surge Capacity Management - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Level 1 Conventional: existing resources, normal standards, +20-30% capacity
rationale: Level 2 Contingency: overflow spaces, equivalent standards, +50-100% capacity | Level 3 Crisis: non-traditional spaces, crisis standards of care, +200% capacity | Crisis standards include resource allocation triage and modified treatment protocols
clinical_pearl: Level 3 crisis surge involves non-traditional spaces AND crisis standards of care with modified treatment protocols; it is not simply adding more beds

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Normal SpO2 in a critically ill patient with metabolic acidosis should raise suspicion for cyanide poisoning; do not be reassured by normal pulse oximetry
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: ABG shows normal PaO2 with severe metabolic acidosis and elevated lactate

front: Clinical decision: Activated Charcoal and Decontamination - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Activated charcoal does not adsorb potassium, iron, or lithium
rationale: WBI: 1-2 L/hour PEG solution via NGT until clear rectal effluent | Simultaneously treat hyperkalemia with standard protocol | Sustained-release tablets continue releasing drug in the GI tract
clinical_pearl: Activated charcoal does NOT adsorb potassium; WBI is the appropriate decontamination method for potassium tablet ingestion

front: Identify calcium oxalate crystalluria as a Stage 2 finding in ethylene glycol toxicity indicating ongoing metabolism to toxic metabolites
back: Stage 2 (cardiopulmonary phase, 12-24 hours post-ingestion), when glycolic acid metabolites are converted to oxalic acid which precipitates as calcium oxalate crystals in the renal tubules
rationale: Ethylene glycol toxicity progresses through three stages: Stage 1 (0-12h): CNS depression mimicking alcohol intoxication, elevated osmolar gap from parent compound; Stage 2 (12-24h): cardiopulmonary effects with tachycardia, mild hypertension, and calcium oxalate crystalluria as glycolic acid is further metabolized to oxalic acid; the oxalic acid binds calcium forming calcium oxalate crystals that deposit in kidneys, brain, and heart; Stage 3 (24-72h): renal failure from calcium oxalate crystal
clinical_pearl: Stage 1: CNS depression, elevated osmolar gap

front: Red Flag: Organophosphate Exposure - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Monitor respiratory muscle strength in all organophosphate poisoning patients for 2-3 weeks; intermediate syndrome respiratory failure can be insidious
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Recovery in 5-18 days as new acetylcholinesterase is synthesized

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Preparedness: planning, training, exercises, coalitions
rationale: Recovery: restoring healthcare system functioning post-event | Emergency Operations Coordination: coalition operations, ESF-8 | Medical Surge: managing patient volume exceeding normal capacity
clinical_pearl: HPP four core capabilities: preparedness, recovery, emergency operations coordination, and medical surge - all coalition-based

front: Differentiate smallpox from varicella by centrifugal distribution, synchronous lesion staging, and palm/sole involvement
back: Smallpox rash is centrifugal (concentrated on face and extremities) with all lesions in the same stage of development; varicella is centripetal (trunk-predominant) with lesions in multiple stages
rationale: The distinction between smallpox and varicella rash distribution is a critical clinical skill because early smallpox cases may be confused with chickenpox, potentially delaying recognition of a bioterrorism event. Key differences include: (1) Distribution: Smallpox is centrifugal - lesions are most concentrated on the face and extremities (including palms and soles) with relatively fewer on the trunk. Varicella is centripetal - lesions are most concentrated on the trunk with fewer on extremities
clinical_pearl: Smallpox: centrifugal distribution (face/extremities)

front: Red Flag: Opioid Overdose and Naloxone - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Lay rescuers should be trained to call 911 before or immediately after naloxone administration; naloxone is not a substitute for emergency medical care
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Community naloxone programs significantly reduce overdose deaths

front: Differentiate SALT from START triage by identifying SALT's global sorting, lifesaving intervention allowance, and five-category system
back: SALT (Sort-Assess-Lifesaving interventions-Treatment/transport) adds a global sorting step and allows limited lifesaving interventions during triage, while START does not permit treatment during the triage process
rationale: SALT (Sort-Assess-Lifesaving interventions-Treatment/transport) triage was developed by the CDC as a more comprehensive alternative to START triage. Key differences include: (1) Global sorting: SALT begins by asking casualties to walk, then wave/have purposeful movement, then assessing the still/obvious life threats. (2) Individual assessment includes limited lifesaving interventions: SALT allows hemorrhage control, opening airway, and two rescue breaths during the triage process itself. In cont
clinical_pearl: SALT: Sort-Assess-Lifesaving interventions-Treatment/transport

front: Clinical decision: Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear (CBRN) Response - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Heated water minimum 100°F/38°C
rationale: Level C PPE minimum (PAPR, chemical suit, boots, gloves) | Runoff containment required | Throughput: 1 ambulatory patient per 3-5 minutes
clinical_pearl: Heated water minimum 100°F; Level C PPE minimum; runoff containment; unidirectional flow; 1 patient per 3-5 min ambulatory, 15-20 min non-ambulatory

front: Clinical decision: Disaster Triage Categories - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: RED area closest to ED with highest staffing ratio
rationale: YELLOW adjacent to RED for easy re-triage if patients deteriorate | GREEN furthest from ED with self-care station | BLACK separated and not visible from other areas or family waiting
clinical_pearl: RED closest to ED for rapid access; GREEN furthest away for self-care; BLACK separated and not visible from other areas; reassess YELLOW every 30 min

front: Recognize visual disturbances as the hallmark and most severe complication of methanol poisoning indicating optic nerve damage
back: Decreased visual acuity and inability to perceive colors, indicating optic nerve damage
rationale: Methanol poisoning is particularly dangerous because its toxic metabolite, formic acid, has specific affinity for the optic nerve and basal ganglia. Formic acid inhibits cytochrome oxidase in the mitochondria of retinal cells and optic nerve, causing optic neuritis, papilledema, and potentially permanent blindness. Visual disturbances including blurred vision, central scotomata, 'snowfield vision' (white spots in visual field), and decreased color perception are hallmark manifestations of methan
clinical_pearl: Methanol's toxic metabolite formic acid targets the optic nerve

front: Differentiate NMS from serotonin syndrome using neuromuscular examination findings as the key distinguishing feature
back: Lead-pipe muscle rigidity with hyporeflexia in NMS versus clonus and hyperreflexia in serotonin syndrome
rationale: The most reliable clinical features distinguishing NMS from serotonin syndrome involve neuromuscular examination findings. NMS characteristically produces 'lead-pipe' rigidity (increased muscle tone that is constant and uniform throughout the range of motion) with hyporeflexia or normal reflexes. In contrast, serotonin syndrome produces clonus (rhythmic involuntary muscle contractions, particularly at the ankles), hyperreflexia, and myoclonus. These neuromuscular findings are the most reliable d
clinical_pearl: NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, hyporeflexia, slow onset (days-weeks), dopamine antagonists

front: Conduct a hot wash immediately after disaster exercises to capture real-time observations for the formal After-Action Report
back: Capture immediate observations, successes, and areas for improvement while memories are fresh, before the formal After-Action Report (AAR) is developed
rationale: A 'hot wash' is an immediate, informal after-action debriefing conducted at the conclusion of a disaster exercise or real event while participants are still on-site. Its primary purpose is to capture real-time observations before memories fade. Key elements include: (1) What went well: Identify successful strategies, effective communication, and good decision-making; (2) What did not go well: Identify breakdowns in communication, resource gaps, procedural failures, and equipment problems; (3) Su
clinical_pearl: Capture observations while memories are fresh

front: Clinical decision: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Centralized patient tracking across multiple hospitals
rationale: Structured death notification protocol with chaplain/social worker | Interpreter services for non-English speaking families | Separate from ED to prevent family interference with patient care
clinical_pearl: FAC: patient tracking, reunification, bereavement, crisis counseling, interpreters, law enforcement liaison; located SEPARATE from ED

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Coral snake neurotoxicity can cause respiratory paralysis with rapid onset once symptoms begin; have intubation equipment ready and monitor for 24 hours minimum
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Antivenom given prophylactically; do not wait for symptoms

front: Recognize argyria as a clinical marker of chronic silver exposure and distinguish it from other metal-induced skin changes
back: Chronic silver exposure (colloidal silver ingestion) causing irreversible argyria - silver deposits in the skin, nails, and mucous membranes
rationale: Argyria is a permanent blue-gray discoloration of the skin caused by chronic exposure to silver or silver compounds. Silver particles deposit in the dermis and are reduced by sunlight exposure, creating the characteristic blue-gray pigmentation that is most prominent in sun-exposed areas. Sources of silver exposure include: (1) Colloidal silver supplements (sold as alternative medicine for various conditions); (2) Silver-containing wound dressings with excessive use; (3) Occupational exposure in
clinical_pearl: Argyria: silver deposits in skin from chronic silver exposure

front: Manage opioid analgesia in tolerant patients by recognizing signs of excess, implementing multimodal analgesia, and monitoring respiratory status
back: Hold the PCA, reassess pain management approach with the physician, consider multimodal analgesia, and monitor respiratory status closely
rationale: This scenario presents the complex challenge of pain management in opioid-tolerant patients. The patient has signs of opioid excess (pinpoint pupils, RR 10) despite reporting inadequate pain relief, which is common in tolerant patients. The appropriate response is to: (1) Hold the PCA to prevent further respiratory depression; (2) Reassess the pain management approach - opioid-tolerant patients often require multimodal analgesia including regional anesthesia, ketamine, gabapentinoids, NSAIDs, an
clinical_pearl: Opioid-tolerant patients need multimodal pain management

front: Clinical decision: Tricyclic Antidepressant Overdose - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Recurrent widening = ongoing absorption or tissue redistribution
rationale: Additional NaHCO3 bolus plus infusion for sustained effect | Target serum pH 7.45-7.55 with bicarbonate infusion | Monitor for at least 24 hours after last QRS widening episode
clinical_pearl: QRS can re-widen hours after initial bicarbonate treatment due to ongoing TCA absorption or tissue redistribution; sustained infusion may be needed

front: Recognize methylene blue as an MAOI that can precipitate serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic medications
back: Methylene blue has MAOI properties that precipitated serotonin syndrome when combined with the SSRI
rationale: Methylene blue has significant monoamine oxidase inhibitory (MAOI) properties that are often overlooked. When administered to a patient already taking serotonergic medications (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, tramadol, meperidine), methylene blue can precipitate serotonin syndrome. The FDA has issued a specific warning about this interaction. This creates a clinical dilemma: the patient needs methylene blue for methemoglobinemia treatment, but the MAOI properties interact dangerously with their chronic SSRI
clinical_pearl: Methylene blue has significant MAOI activity

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: DDAVP releases vWF to enhance platelet adhesion independent of blocked pathways
rationale: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1; clopidogrel irreversibly blocks P2Y12 | Wait 4-6 hours after last clopidogrel dose before platelet transfusion | Affected platelets remain dysfunctional for their 7-10 day lifespan
clinical_pearl: Normal platelet count does not rule out platelet dysfunction; antiplatelet drugs cause qualitative not quantitative defects

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Pediatric laundry pod ingestion deaths have occurred; these products are significantly more dangerous than traditional detergents
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: No ipecac or activated charcoal for caustic substances

front: Manage spontaneous volunteers through structured credentialing, orientation, and supervised assignment to prevent operational disruption and ensure patient safety
back: Unaffiliated volunteers may lack proper credentials, training, or immunizations; they can overwhelm operations, create liability issues, and potentially cause harm; hospitals need a volunteer reception center to credential, orient, assign, and supervise spontaneous volunteers under the Emergency System for Advance Registration of Volunteer Health Professionals (ESAR-VHP)
rationale: Spontaneous volunteer management during disasters: (1) Challenge: well-meaning individuals arrive at disaster sites and hospitals wanting to help but without coordination, credentials, or training; this can overwhelm operations and create safety/liability concerns; (2) ESAR-VHP: the Emergency System for Advance Registration of Volunteer Health Professionals is a federal program that pre-registers and credentials health professionals for emergency deployment; volunteers registered in ESAR-VHP hav
clinical_pearl: Unaffiliated volunteers may lack credentials, training, or immunizations

front: Clinical decision: Opioid Overdose and Naloxone - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Buprenorphine has Ki 0.22 nM, requiring 10-15+ mg naloxone for displacement
rationale: Partial reversal may be the best achievable outcome | Continuous high-dose naloxone infusion may be needed | Co-ingestants (alcohol) compound respiratory depression
clinical_pearl: Buprenorphine has higher mu-receptor binding affinity than naloxone; standard 0.4-2 mg doses are often ineffective

front: Clinical decision: Psychological First Aid (PFA) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: 8 core elements: contact, safety, stabilization, info gathering, practical help, connections, coping, services
rationale: Validate emotional responses; normalize acute stress reactions | PFA is evidence-informed, not formal mental health treatment | Address practical needs: food, water, shelter, medications, family contact
clinical_pearl: PFA is NOT psychotherapy; it is practical, immediate support. Validate emotions, ensure safety, meet practical needs, and connect with resources

front: Perform dynamic re-triage during mass casualty incidents when patient conditions change
back: Re-triage the patient to RED (Immediate) and move them to the immediate treatment area for priority care
rationale: Disaster triage is a dynamic, continuous process, not a one-time assessment. Patients can move between triage categories in either direction as their clinical condition changes. A YELLOW patient who develops respiratory distress requires immediate re-triage to RED status and transfer to the immediate treatment area. The principle of ongoing reassessment is fundamental to effective disaster triage management. Recommended reassessment intervals are: RED patients every 15 minutes, YELLOW patients e
clinical_pearl: Reassessment intervals: RED q15min, YELLOW q30min, GREEN q60min

front: Clinical decision: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Pre-verbal children cannot self-identify
rationale: Photo documentation critical; appearance changes rapidly | DNA buccal swab for forensic matching if needed | Verify custody and involve CPS for unaccompanied children
clinical_pearl: Photograph every unidentified child; document clothing and distinguishing marks; DNA buccal swab; verify custody before release; CPS for unaccompanied children

front: Red Flag: Blast Injury Patterns - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Without pre-treatment, extrication after prolonged entrapment can cause immediate cardiac arrest from hyperkalemia; ALWAYS pre-treat if IV access is possible
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Sequence: fluids → calcium → bicarb → release → continue fluids

front: Red Flag: Pandemic Response Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Novel respiratory pathogens may have asymptomatic or presymptomatic transmission; screening must cast a wide net based on epidemiological criteria, not just symptoms
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Separate patient flow pathways for respiratory vs non-respiratory patients

front: Clinical decision: Opioid Overdose and Naloxone - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Carfentanil: 10,000x morphine, 100x fentanyl potency
rationale: Dermal absorption and inhalation can cause rescuer overdose | N95 minimum, double nitrile gloves, eye protection required | Much higher naloxone doses needed (10+ mg)
clinical_pearl: Carfentanil is 10,000x morphine potency; invisible quantities can be fatal through skin absorption or inhalation, putting healthcare workers at risk

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Agranulocytosis: ANC <500; severe agranulocytosis: ANC <200
rationale: Febrile neutropenia is a medical emergency requiring empiric antibiotics | Clozapine requires mandatory ANC monitoring through REMS program | G-CSF (filgrastim) stimulates neutrophil production
clinical_pearl: Clozapine-induced agranulocytosis requires permanent discontinuation; the drug can NEVER be restarted after agranulocytosis

front: Determine that NAC dosing does not require adjustment for renal impairment while monitoring fluid volume in CKD patients
back: Standard NAC dosing is used; NAC is hepatically metabolized and renal impairment does not significantly affect its pharmacokinetics or efficacy
rationale: NAC (N-acetylcysteine) is primarily metabolized hepatically and its pharmacokinetics are not significantly affected by renal impairment. Standard dosing should be used for patients with chronic kidney disease at any stage. However, there are specific considerations for CKD patients with acetaminophen toxicity: (1) Volume of dilution should be adjusted for patients at risk of fluid overload; (2) CKD patients may have lower baseline glutathione levels, potentially making them more susceptible to a
clinical_pearl: NAC hepatically metabolized; standard dosing in CKD

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Horizontal: same floor, behind fire doors, fastest option
rationale: Vertical: between floors, 2-4 staff per bed-bound patient | Total: complete evacuation to external receiving facilities | Ambulatory patients evacuated first (require least assistance)
clinical_pearl: Horizontal = same floor behind fire doors (fastest); vertical = between floors (2-4 staff per bed-bound patient); total = complete building evacuation to external facilities

front: Recognize fulminant hepatic failure from acetaminophen toxicity and initiate transplant evaluation using King's College Criteria
back: Fulminant hepatic failure with coagulopathy requiring hepatology consultation for possible liver transplant evaluation and King's College Criteria assessment
rationale: Rapidly rising AST (200 to 8,500) with worsening coagulopathy (INR 1.2 to 4.8) indicates fulminant hepatic failure from severe acetaminophen toxicity. The liver is failing to produce clotting factors (hence rising INR) and hepatocytes are being destroyed (rising AST). This trajectory requires immediate hepatology consultation for liver transplant evaluation. The King's College Criteria for acetaminophen-induced fulminant hepatic failure include: arterial pH <7.3 regardless of other factors, OR a
clinical_pearl: AST >10,000 with rising INR = fulminant hepatic failure

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: 5 phases: GI → apparent recovery → hepatotoxicity → hepatic failure → GI scarring
rationale: Phase 2 apparent recovery is deceptive and dangerous | Deferoxamine: chelates free iron; vin rosé colored urine | Phase 3 = direct mitochondrial toxicity causing metabolic acidosis
clinical_pearl: Phase 2 (6-24h) apparent recovery is the exam trap - patients look better but iron is being absorbed into organs; Phase 3 is the most lethal

front: Red Flag: Toxic Ingestion in Pediatrics - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Transdermal medication patches pose extreme danger to small children; even used patches retain significant amounts of active drug
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Clonidine patches continue to release medication in GI tract

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Members: hospitals, LTC, EMS, public health, emergency management
rationale: Functions: joint planning, training, resource sharing, communication | No single facility can manage large disasters alone | HPP cooperative agreements fund coalition activities
clinical_pearl: Healthcare coalitions are multi-organizational partnerships (not just hospitals); no single facility can manage large disasters alone; coalition approach pools resources and capabilities

front: Red Flag: Incident Command System (ICS) - What safety concern must you remember?
back: During MCI, nursing leadership must balance patient care demands with staff safety; fatigued, distressed nurses are a patient safety risk
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Monitor staff welfare and arrange relief for fatigue/distress

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Respiratory failure from diaphragm paralysis can occur rapidly; have intubation equipment ready and monitor respiratory function closely
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Notify public health for source control

front: Diagnose ethylene glycol poisoning by the triad of anion gap metabolic acidosis, elevated osmolar gap, and calcium oxalate crystalluria
back: Ethylene glycol, which is metabolized to glycolic acid (causing acidosis) and oxalic acid (causing calcium oxalate crystal deposition in kidneys)
rationale: Ethylene glycol (the main component of antifreeze) poisoning is confirmed by the combination of severe anion gap metabolic acidosis, elevated osmolar gap, and calcium oxalate crystals in the urine. Ethylene glycol is metabolized through alcohol dehydrogenase to glycoaldehyde, then glycolic acid (responsible for the metabolic acidosis and elevated anion gap), and finally to oxalic acid, which combines with calcium to form calcium oxalate crystals. These crystals deposit in the renal tubules causi
clinical_pearl: EG metabolites: glycolic acid (acidosis) and oxalic acid (crystals)

front: Administer phenobarbital as rescue therapy for benzodiazepine-resistant alcohol withdrawal seizures
back: IV phenobarbital loading dose of 10-20 mg/kg as rescue therapy for benzodiazepine-resistant alcohol withdrawal seizures
rationale: Benzodiazepine-resistant alcohol withdrawal occurs when escalating doses of benzodiazepines fail to control symptoms, typically defined as requiring >40 mg diazepam equivalents per hour or >200 mg in 24 hours without adequate symptom control. This represents a true pharmacological failure of the GABA-A benzodiazepine binding site. Phenobarbital is the preferred rescue agent because it acts at a different site on the GABA-A receptor (the barbiturate binding site), providing additive GABAergic eff
clinical_pearl: BZD-resistant: >40mg diazepam equiv/hour or >200mg/24h without control

front: Fulfill the Nursing Unit Leader role in HIMT by managing nursing assignments, staffing ratios, and care quality during MCI
back: Manage nursing staff assignments, track nurse-to-patient ratios, communicate staffing needs to the Medical Branch Director, and ensure nursing care quality despite crisis conditions
rationale: The Nursing Unit Leader in the Hospital Incident Management Team (HIMT) is responsible for the tactical management of nursing resources during the incident. Key responsibilities include: (1) Nursing staff assignments: deploy nurses to treatment areas based on acuity, skill level, and staffing ratios; (2) Staffing ratio monitoring: track and maintain safe nurse-to-patient ratios, adjusting as patient volume and acuity change; (3) Communication: report staffing needs, patient care concerns, and re
clinical_pearl: Manage nursing staff assignments by acuity and skill level

front: Clinical decision: Toxic Alcohol Poisoning - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Fomepizole blocks alcohol dehydrogenase preventing toxic metabolite formation
rationale: Ethylene glycol itself is non-toxic; metabolites cause organ damage | Give empirically based on history; do not wait for levels | Loading dose: 15 mg/kg IV, then 10 mg/kg q12h
clinical_pearl: Do NOT wait for confirmatory levels to give fomepizole; ethylene glycol metabolism begins immediately and earlier treatment prevents more toxic metabolite formation

front: Clinical decision: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Fetal hemoglobin has higher CO affinity than adult hemoglobin
rationale: Fetal COHb levels are 10-15% higher than maternal levels | HBO threshold is lower in pregnancy due to fetal vulnerability | HBO is recommended for all pregnant patients with significant CO exposure
clinical_pearl: Fetal COHb is 10-15% higher than maternal levels with longer elimination; always treat pregnant CO patients more aggressively

front: Red Flag: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Medications are the most commonly forgotten critical item; ensure patients maintain an adequate emergency supply of all prescription medications
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Review kit every 6 months; rotate perishable items

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Insulin improves myocardial glucose uptake via GLUT4
rationale: Doses far exceed normal therapeutic range (up to 10 u/kg/h) | Blood glucose every 15-30 minutes initially | Replace potassium to maintain K+ >3.0 mEq/L
clinical_pearl: HIET: insulin 1 unit/kg bolus then 1-10 units/kg/h; glucose q15-30min; K+ q1-2h; D10-25W infusion to maintain euglycemia; improves cardiac contractility

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Thyroid storm mortality is 20-30% even with treatment; immediate multi-modal therapy and ICU monitoring are essential
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Hydrocortisone treats relative adrenal insufficiency and blocks T4-to-T3 conversion

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Pre-printed paper forms in downtime kits on all units
rationale: Manual MAR reconciled with last-known electronic MAR | Manual bed board for patient tracking | Data reconciliation plan for post-restoration entry
clinical_pearl: Downtime kits pre-stocked on all units with paper forms; annual practice of downtime procedures; data reconciliation plan for EHR restoration

front: Clinical decision: Acetaminophen Overdose and NAC Protocol - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: AST >10,000 with rising INR = fulminant hepatic failure
rationale: King's College Criteria guide transplant evaluation | Continue NAC even in established hepatic failure | Arrange emergent transfer to liver transplant center
clinical_pearl: King's College Criteria: pH <7.3 alone OR (INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4 + Grade 3-4 encephalopathy) predicts >90% mortality without transplant

front: Red Flag: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Releasing a child to an unverified adult during MCI chaos can result in custody violations, criminal exploitation, or permanent family separation
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Media management: prevent media access to reunification area

front: Recognize inhalational anthrax by its characteristic widened mediastinum and hemorrhagic effusions following white powder exposure
back: Inhalational anthrax (Bacillus anthracis)
rationale: The clinical presentation of widened mediastinum, hemorrhagic pleural effusions, and rapid deterioration after exposure to a white powder delivered in envelopes is classic for inhalational anthrax caused by Bacillus anthracis spores. Inhalational anthrax was a bioterrorism reality in the 2001 postal attacks. The clinical progression follows two phases: (1) Prodromal phase (1-5 days): nonspecific flu-like symptoms including fever, malaise, myalgias, and nonproductive cough. (2) Fulminant phase (r
clinical_pearl: Widened mediastinum is the hallmark of inhalational anthrax

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Broselow-Luten system: color-coded, length-based equipment and dosing
rationale: Must be unlocked, accessible, and familiar to all staff | Joint Commission and EMSC recommend pediatric equipment in all EDs | Regular inspection and restocking required
clinical_pearl: Pediatric emergency equipment must be immediately accessible and organized; locked equipment is functionally nonexistent during a crisis

front: Clinical decision: Anticholinergic Toxidrome - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Pharmacological: pupil does NOT respond to pilocarpine
rationale: Neurological: pupil WILL respond to pilocarpine | Common topical mydriatics: tropicamide, phenylephrine, atropine | History of eye drop use is the most important initial assessment
clinical_pearl: Pilocarpine test differentiates pharmacological mydriasis (won't constrict) from neurological mydriasis (will constrict); prevents unnecessary neurosurgical intervention

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Sudden sniffing death can occur with first-time inhalant use
rationale: Halogenated hydrocarbons sensitize myocardium to catecholamines | Use epinephrine cautiously in ACLS for inhalant-induced VF | Beta-blockers may help with refractory dysrhythmias from inhalant abuse
clinical_pearl: Exogenous epinephrine should be used cautiously in inhalant-induced VF as it may worsen catecholamine-sensitized dysrhythmias

front: Establish temporary morgue operations with appropriate refrigerated storage, security, documentation, and medicolegal chain of custody during disaster situations
back: Designated refrigerated trucks or trailers obtained through emergency management channels, with appropriate security, documentation, and chain-of-custody procedures for medicolegal purposes; temporary morgue area must be separate from patient care and family areas
rationale: Temporary morgue management during disasters requires careful planning: (1) Refrigerated storage: ideal temp 34-38°F (1-3°C); refrigerated trucks/trailers from food service companies, FEMA, or funeral homes; (2) Location: separate from patient care areas (both for infection control and psychological impact); separate from family waiting/reunification areas; secured to prevent unauthorized access; accessible for body transfer to transport vehicles; (3) Documentation: each body receives a unique i
clinical_pearl: Refrigerated trucks/trailers from FEMA or food service companies

front: Recognize that post-DigiFab digoxin levels are falsely elevated and clinical response guides management, not drug levels
back: No, the elevated level represents antibody-bound (inactive) digoxin; clinical response, not drug levels, should guide further dosing
rationale: After DigiFab administration, total serum digoxin levels become unreliable for clinical decision-making because standard digoxin assays measure both free (active) and antibody-bound (inactive) digoxin. DigiFab binds free digoxin in the serum, rendering it pharmacologically inactive, but the antibody-digoxin complex is measured by standard assays, causing falsely elevated total digoxin levels that can be 10-20 times the pre-treatment level. This phenomenon can persist for days to weeks as the ant
clinical_pearl: Post-DigiFab levels falsely elevated: assay measures bound + free digoxin

front: Clinical decision: Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear (CBRN) Response - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Outer gloves first (most contaminated)
rationale: Suit rolled inside-out without touching outer surface | Respirator/PAPR removed LAST for continued protection | Buddy system mandatory; hand hygiene between steps
clinical_pearl: Respirator/PAPR is removed LAST during doffing; outer gloves first (most contaminated); buddy system for safe removal; hand hygiene between each step

front: Red Flag: Pandemic Response Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Placing respiratory patients in unmodified standard rooms without airborne precautions causes nosocomial transmission to other patients and staff
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Verify modifications with smoke testing or differential pressure

front: Establish casualty collection point placement based on safety, proximity, access, space, and communication requirements for efficient MCI patient management
back: Upwind and uphill from the incident, outside the hazard zone but close enough for efficient patient transport, with adequate space, vehicle access for ambulances, weather protection, and clear marking visible to rescue teams
rationale: Casualty Collection Point (CCP) placement requires strategic consideration of multiple factors: (1) Safety: upwind and uphill from the incident (especially important for HAZMAT, fire); outside any established hazard zones; protected from secondary hazards (structural collapse, secondary devices in bombing); (2) Proximity: close enough to the incident for efficient patient transport but far enough for safety; (3) Access: clear vehicle access for ambulances and evacuation vehicles; multiple entry/
clinical_pearl: Upwind and uphill from incident

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: All patients with confirmed coral snake bites should receive antivenom prophylactically due to the potential for delayed, rapidly progressive, and potentially irreversible neurotoxicity
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: CroFab is for pit vipers only; coral snakes need Micrurus fulvius antivenin

front: Red Flag: Digoxin Toxicity - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Do not administer additional DigiFab based on post-treatment digoxin levels alone; this wastes an expensive resource and is not clinically indicated
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Free digoxin assays are available but not universally

front: Apply JumpSTART pediatric respiratory rate thresholds (15-45/min) that differ from adult START thresholds
back: A respiratory rate below 15 in JumpSTART is classified as RED (Immediate), as the normal pediatric range used is 15-45 breaths per minute
rationale: JumpSTART triage uses a different respiratory rate range than adult START triage. In JumpSTART, the normal respiratory rate range is 15-45 breaths per minute for children. A rate below 15 or above 45 is abnormal and the child is classified as RED (Immediate). This 4-year-old breathing at 10/min is below the JumpSTART lower threshold of 15, indicating respiratory compromise requiring immediate intervention. In contrast, adult START uses <30 as the normal threshold. The lower threshold in JumpSTAR
clinical_pearl: JumpSTART RR range: 15-45/min (vs adult START: <30)

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: IV vitamin K must be administered slowly (over 10-20 minutes) due to risk of anaphylactoid reactions; oral vitamin K takes 12-24 hours to work
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: PCC does not require blood typing or thawing time

front: Recognize that undetectable acetaminophen levels do not rule out severe hepatotoxicity and that NAC should be given for fulminant hepatic failure
back: Initiate IV NAC immediately and consult hepatology for possible liver transplant evaluation
rationale: This patient presents with fulminant hepatic failure from acetaminophen toxicity, evidenced by jaundice, coagulopathy (INR 4.8), and encephalopathy (hepatic encephalopathy). The undetectable acetaminophen level does not rule out severe toxicity; rather, it indicates that the acetaminophen has already been completely metabolized, and the liver damage is from the toxic metabolite NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine), which has already been formed and caused hepatocellular necrosis. At this stage,
clinical_pearl: Undetectable APAP level with liver failure indicates complete metabolism and severe toxicity

front: Recognize cumulative anticholinergic burden from polypharmacy and use validated screening tools to quantify risk
back: Anticholinergic Burden Scale (ABS) or Anticholinergic Risk Scale (ARS) to quantify the cumulative effect of multiple anticholinergic medications
rationale: This patient is experiencing anticholinergic toxicity not from a single overdose but from the cumulative anticholinergic burden of multiple medications with anticholinergic properties. Oxybutynin (urinary antispasmodic), diphenhydramine (antihistamine), and amitriptyline (tricyclic antidepressant) all have significant anticholinergic effects. The concept of anticholinergic burden recognizes that even therapeutic doses of multiple anticholinergic medications can produce toxicity when their effect
clinical_pearl: Multiple medications with anticholinergic properties have additive effects

front: Manage staggered acetaminophen ingestion when the Rumack-Matthew nomogram is inapplicable
back: Start NAC immediately based on clinical evidence of hepatotoxicity regardless of nomogram applicability
rationale: For staggered or chronic acetaminophen ingestions, the Rumack-Matthew nomogram is unreliable because it was designed for single acute ingestions with known timing. In these cases, the decision to treat with NAC should be based on clinical criteria: elevated transaminases (AST 450, ALT 380 in this case), detectable acetaminophen level, or total dose exceeding 150 mg/kg over a 24-hour period. This patient's total dose of 12g over 8 hours in a typical adult exceeds the toxic threshold. NAC should b
clinical_pearl: Nomogram only valid for single acute ingestion with known timing

front: Red Flag: Blast Injury Patterns - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Field providers must have cardiac monitoring and potassium-lowering treatments ready before extrication; 'rescue death' from hyperkalemia is a recognized phenomenon
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: IV calcium gluconate for cardiac protection from hyperkalemia

front: Clinical decision: Toxic Ingestion in Pediatrics - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Metformin does not stimulate insulin release so does not cause hypoglycemia
rationale: Primary toxicity is lactic acidosis from mitochondrial dysfunction | Observe 12-24 hours with serial lactate and BMP monitoring | Hemodialysis effectively removes metformin if lactic acidosis develops
clinical_pearl: Metformin does NOT cause hypoglycemia; the danger is delayed lactic acidosis from mitochondrial complex I inhibition

front: Manage MAOI-tyramine hypertensive crisis with rapidly acting parenteral alpha-blocking agents
back: IV phentolamine or nitroprusside for acute hypertensive emergency
rationale: This patient is experiencing a tyramine-induced hypertensive crisis, a well-known and potentially lethal interaction between monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) and tyramine-rich foods. MAOIs inhibit monoamine oxidase in the gut and liver, which normally metabolizes dietary tyramine. When tyramine is not metabolized, it enters systemic circulation and displaces stored catecholamines (norepinephrine, dopamine) from presynaptic vesicles, causing a massive sympathomimetic surge with severe hyperte
clinical_pearl: MAOIs prevent tyramine metabolism allowing massive catecholamine release

front: Clinical decision: Disaster Triage Categories - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: RED reassessment every 15 minutes; YELLOW every 30 minutes
rationale: Treatment priority: RED -> YELLOW -> GREEN -> BLACK (comfort care only) | GREEN patients need a holding area with periodic re-triage | Maintain communication with IC about patient flow and resource needs
clinical_pearl: Triage is dynamic, not static; RED patients need reassessment every 15 minutes and can change categories in either direction

front: Implement high-dose insulin euglycemia therapy as rescue treatment for refractory calcium channel blocker toxicity
back: High-dose insulin euglycemia therapy (HIET) with regular insulin 1 unit/kg bolus followed by 1-10 units/kg/hour infusion
rationale: This patient has severe calcium channel blocker (CCB) toxicity from sustained-release verapamil, presenting with profound bradycardia, hypotension, and paradoxical hyperglycemia. The hyperglycemia is actually a hallmark of CCB toxicity because calcium channel blockade in pancreatic beta cells impairs insulin secretion, creating a state of insulin deficiency. High-dose insulin euglycemia therapy (HIET) has emerged as a crucial rescue therapy for CCB and beta-blocker toxicity refractory to standar
clinical_pearl: HIET: insulin 1 unit/kg bolus then 1-10 units/kg/hour infusion

front: Manage antiplatelet drug-related bleeding with DDAVP for platelet adhesion enhancement and platelet transfusion for severe bleeding
back: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.3 mcg/kg IV to enhance platelet adhesion and consider platelet transfusion
rationale: This patient has bleeding from antiplatelet drug-induced platelet dysfunction rather than thrombocytopenia (his platelet count is normal). Dual antiplatelet therapy (clopidogrel + aspirin) irreversibly inhibits platelet function through different mechanisms: aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) preventing thromboxane A2 formation, and clopidogrel irreversibly blocks the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets. Since these effects are irreversible, the affected platelets will remain dys
clinical_pearl: DDAVP releases vWF to enhance platelet adhesion independent of blocked pathways

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Pre-verbal children cannot provide name, age, or medical history
