rationale: Activated charcoal is most effective when administered within 1-2 hours of ingestion of a substance that is adsorbed by charcoal, in a patient with an intact airway protective reflexes. The ideal candidate is the 30-year-old with carbamazepine ingestion because: (1) Carbamazepine is well-adsorbed by activated charcoal, (2) the patient presented within the optimal window of 45 minutes, (3) the patient is alert with intact airway reflexes (reducing aspiration risk), and (4) carbamazepine undergoes
clinical_pearl: Activated charcoal most effective within 1-2 hours of ingestion

front: Clinical decision: Serotonin Syndrome - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Methylene blue has significant MAOI activity
rationale: FDA has issued a Drug Safety Communication about this interaction | Alternative methemoglobinemia treatments: ascorbic acid, exchange transfusion | Always review medication list for serotonergic drugs before methylene blue

front: Clinical decision: Incident Command System (ICS) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: FEMA requires detailed documentation for reimbursement eligibility

clinical_pearl: Detailed resource tracking during MCI enables FEMA reimbursement under the Stafford Act; without documentation, hospitals lose potential reimbursement

front: Clinical decision: Pandemic Response Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?

back: Positive pressure ventilation in blast lung increases air embolism and pneumothorax risk; use the lowest effective pressures and watch for acute decompensation

front: Clinical decision: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What is the key clinical pearl?

front: Monitor pediatric metformin ingestion for delayed lactic acidosis development regardless of normal glucose levels
back: Metformin does not typically cause hypoglycemia but can cause lactic acidosis which may develop on a delayed basis; the child needs observation and serial monitoring
rationale: Metformin toxicity presents differently from other diabetic medication overdoses because metformin does not directly cause hypoglycemia (it works by reducing hepatic glucose production and improving insulin sensitivity, not by stimulating insulin release). The primary concern in metformin ingestion is lactic acidosis, which can develop on a delayed basis as the drug accumulates and inhibits mitochondrial complex I. clinical_pearl: Metformin does not stimulate insulin release so does not cause hypoglycemia

front: Address pediatric-specific CBRN vulnerabilities including increased agent susceptibility, decontamination challenges, weight-based dosing, and hypothermia risk
back: Children have higher minute ventilation per body mass (increased inhalation exposure), thinner skin (increased dermal absorption), shorter stature (exposure to heavier-than-air agents settling near ground), smaller fluid reserves (faster decompensation), and limited ability to self-decontaminate or follow instructions
rationale: Pediatric CBRN vulnerabilities: (1) Respiratory: higher minute ventilation relative to body mass means children inhale proportionally more aerosolized agent per kilogram; (2) Dermal: thinner skin with greater surface area-to-mass ratio increases percutaneous absorption of chemical and radiological agents; (3) Height: shorter stature places children closer to the ground where heavier-than-air chemical agents (chlorine, sarin, VX) concentrate; (4) Fluid reserves: smaller blood volume and limited g
clinical_pearl: Higher minute ventilation: more inhalation exposure per kg

back: Organophosphate or carbamate insecticide exposure causing cholinergic crisis with SLUDGE symptoms and bradycardia
rationale: Miosis (pinpoint pupils) has a broader differential diagnosis than just opioid use. Cholinergic excess from organophosphate/carbamate poisoning also causes miosis as part of the muscarinic effects (SLUDGE/DUMBELS). clinical_pearl: OP miosis: + SLUDGE + bradycardia + bronchorrhea

front: Apply whole bowel irrigation for sustained-release medication overdoses to mechanically flush intact tablets before full drug release occurs

rationale: Whole bowel irrigation (WBI) using polyethylene glycol-electrolyte solution (GoLYTELY) is a GI decontamination technique that mechanically flushes intact pills through the GI tract. clinical_pearl: SR tablets release drug over 12-24h, overwhelming charcoal capacity

front: Clinical decision: Cholinergic Toxidrome - What is the key clinical pearl?

clinical_pearl: Glucagon activates adenylate cyclase independent of beta-receptors

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: INH depletes B6 needed for GABA synthesis causing seizures

front: Red Flag: Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear (CBRN) Response - What safety concern must you remember?

front: Clinical decision: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: YELLOW: non-ambulatory but stable vital parameters and mental status

front: Establish redundant communication capabilities for MCI response including radio, satellite, HAM, and landline systems when cellular networks fail

clinical_pearl: Hospital UHF/VHF radio for internal communication

back: An outdated EOP is worse than no plan; inaccurate contact information, resource lists, and procedures create false confidence and dangerous gaps

rationale: NIMS (National Incident Management System) key principles for hospitals: (1) INCIDENT COMMAND SYSTEM (ICS): standardized management structure with defined roles, span of control (3-7 per supervisor), modular organization that expands/contracts based on incident needs; (2) COMMON TERMINOLOGY: standardized language across agencies prevents confusion (e.g., everyone calls it 'Staging Area' not 'marshaling area' or 'assembly point'); (3) UNIFIED COMMAND: when multiple jurisdictions or agencies are i

front: Manage brown recluse spider envenomation with conservative wound care and monitoring, avoiding early surgical excision

The classic presentation is a 'red, white, and blue' sign: central pallor or necrosis (white/blue), surrounded by erythema (red). Management is primarily conservative: wound care with gentle cleaning, pain management, tetanus prophylaxis if not current, and close follow-up for wound monitoring every 48-72 hours. Daps
clinical_pearl: Brown recluse venom contains sphingomyelinase D causing dermonecrosis

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Mark swelling edge with pen and time every 15-30 min
rationale: Labs: CBC, PT/INR, fibrinogen, CMP, CK | Opioids safe for pain; avoid NSAIDs | Eastern diamondback: most dangerous North American pit viper

front: Manage secondary blast injuries as penetrating trauma with hemorrhage control, imaging for retained fragments, wound care, and infection prevention

rationale: Blast injuries are classified into four categories: Primary (direct blast wave effect on air-filled organs), Secondary (projectile/fragment injuries), Tertiary (body displacement injuries), and Quaternary (all other injuries including burns, crush, inhalation). Secondary blast injuries are the MOST COMMON cause of blast-related death and injury. They are caused by: shrapnel from the explosive device (nails, ball bearings intentionally packed in IEDs), fragments of objects in the environment (gla
clinical_pearl: Most common cause of blast-related death

front: Red Flag: Pandemic Response Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Screen all disaster shelter residents for chronic medication needs during intake; medication interruption causes more illness and death than the disaster injuries in many events

clinical_pearl: DKA can develop within days of insulin interruption

front: Clinical decision: Serotonin Syndrome - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Tyramine in aged foods normally metabolized by intestinal MAO
rationale: MAOI inhibition allows tyramine to cause massive NE release | Hypertensive crisis can cause intracranial hemorrhage | Treatment: phentolamine, nitroprusside, labetalol; avoid sole beta-blockers

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?

front: Apply the first step of START triage by classifying all ambulatory patients as GREEN regardless of visible injuries

rationale: In the START triage system, the very first step is to assess whether the patient can walk. Any patient who is ambulatory (able to walk independently to the designated area) is automatically classified as GREEN (Minor), regardless of visible injuries. This is because the ability to ambulate demonstrates adequate perfusion, oxygenation, and neurological function to sustain independent movement. The large laceration with controlled bleeding, while requiring treatment, does not change the GREEN clas
clinical_pearl: First START triage question: Can the patient walk?

front: Red Flag: Serotonin Syndrome - What safety concern must you remember?

front: Recognize hydrogen sulfide toxicity and identify appropriate antidotal therapy based on its mechanism of cellular asphyxia
back: Hydroxocobalamin or nitrite-based therapy as a cyanide/sulfide antidote
rationale: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is a highly toxic gas that smells like rotten eggs at low concentrations but causes olfactory fatigue at higher levels. H2S toxicity mechanism is similar to cyanide poisoning - it inhibits cytochrome c oxidase in the mitochondrial electron transport chain, causing cellular asphyxia despite adequate oxygen delivery. This results in cellular hypoxia with a normal PaO2 but inability to utilize oxygen (histotoxic hypoxia). Treatment includes high-flow 100% oxygen (already bein
clinical_pearl: H2S inhibits cytochrome c oxidase similar to cyanide

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Hyperthermia above 104°F from sympathomimetic toxicity is immediately life-threatening; begin aggressive cooling before other interventions

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Classic 'red, white, and blue' concentric wound pattern

front: Use the osmolar gap to screen for toxic alcohol ingestion and differentiate from ethanol intoxication
back: An osmolar gap calculation: a significantly elevated osmolar gap (>10) with the patient appearing intoxicated suggests methanol or ethylene glycol rather than ethanol
rationale: The osmolar gap is a valuable bedside tool for differentiating toxic alcohol (methanol, ethylene glycol) intoxication from ethanol intoxication. The osmolar gap is calculated as: measured serum osmolality minus calculated osmolality (2[Na] + glucose/18 + BUN/2.8 + ethanol/4.6). A gap greater than 10 mOsm/kg suggests the presence of unmeasured osmoles from toxic alcohols. In methanol or ethylene glycol poisoning, the parent alcohol contributes to the osmolar gap early in the course, while the tox
clinical_pearl: Osmolar gap >10 suggests unmeasured osmoles from toxic alcohols

front: Clinical decision: Acetaminophen Overdose and NAC Protocol - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: NAC crosses the placenta providing fetal hepatoprotection
rationale: Fetal liver has limited detoxification capacity making it vulnerable to NAPQI | Standard NAC dosing is used without modification in pregnancy | Untreated APAP toxicity carries risk of fetal hepatotoxicity and fetal death
clinical_pearl: NAC is safe in pregnancy and crosses the placenta to protect the fetal liver; never withhold or reduce the dose

front: Clinical decision: Acetaminophen Overdose and NAC Protocol - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Nomogram only valid for single acute ingestion with known timing
rationale: Staggered ingestions: treat based on clinical evidence of hepatotoxicity | Total dose >150 mg/kg over 24 hours warrants NAC regardless | Continue NAC until transaminases trending down and INR normalizing
clinical_pearl: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is ONLY valid for single acute ingestions with known timing; staggered ingestions require clinical decision-making

front: Recognize the deceptive Phase II of Amanita phalloides poisoning and prevent premature discharge
back: No, the apparent recovery phase (Phase II) of Amanita phalloides poisoning is deceptively benign; fulminant hepatic failure (Phase III) typically develops in 3-5 days
rationale: Amanita phalloides poisoning follows a triphasic course: Phase I (6-24h) GI symptoms, Phase II (24-48h) apparent recovery with deceptively normal labs, Phase III (3-5d) fulminant hepatic failure. The apparent improvement in Phase II leads to premature discharge, which can be fatal. Treatment includes NAC for hepatoprotection, silibinin or penicillin G, aggressive fluids, and hepatology consultation for possible transplant evaluation. Serial liver function tests every 6-12 hours are mandatory.
clinical_pearl: Amanita poisoning: Phase I GI, Phase II apparent recovery, Phase III hepatic failure

front: Clinical decision: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Smaller blood volume: faster hemorrhagic decompensation
rationale: Larger BSA-to-weight ratio: hypothermia and toxicant risk | Weight-based dosing with Broselow tape | Family reunification and family-centered care are priorities
clinical_pearl: Children decompensate faster from blood loss, absorb more toxicants per kg, and have higher hypothermia risk; Broselow tape is essential for weight-based dosing

front: Red Flag: Heavy Metal Poisoning - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Colloidal silver supplements are marketed with unproven health claims; argyria is a permanent cosmetic consequence that patients are rarely warned about

front: Describe DMORT composition, functions, and deployment for mass fatality management including victim identification and family assistance
back: DMORT is a federal team of forensic experts (pathologists, dentists, anthropologists, funeral directors, mental health professionals) that assists local jurisdictions with victim identification, morgue operations, and family assistance during mass fatality events
rationale: DMORT (Disaster Mortuary Operational Response Team) is a component of the National Disaster Medical System (NDMS) under HHS/ASPR: (1) Composition: forensic pathologists, forensic anthropologists, forensic dentists (odontologists), DNA specialists, funeral directors, medical investigators, X-ray technicians, mental health professionals, administrative and logistics support; (2) Functions: victim identification through dental records, fingerprints, DNA, and personal effects; temporary morgue estab
clinical_pearl: Forensic experts: pathologists, dentists, anthropologists, DNA specialists

front: Recognize linezolid as a commonly overlooked MAOI that can precipitate serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic medications
back: Discontinue linezolid immediately, notify the physician, and prepare cyproheptadine for probable serotonin syndrome caused by the SSRI-MAOI combination
rationale: This is a classic serotonin syndrome presentation caused by the combination of an SSRI (sertraline) with a medication possessing MAOI properties (linezolid). Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibiotic that has significant monoamine oxidase inhibition, creating dangerous serotonin excess when combined with serotonergic medications. The triad of agitation, clonus, and diaphoresis is characteristic of serotonin syndrome (Hunter Criteria). Other common precipitating combinations include: SSRI + tramad
clinical_pearl: Linezolid has significant MAO inhibition activity

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Water: 1 gallon per person per day for 3+ days
rationale: Medications: 7-day supply with prescription copies | Cash in small bills; ATMs may be non-functional | Review kit every 6 months; rotate perishable items
clinical_pearl: FEMA: 1 gallon water/person/day for 3 days; 7-day medication supply; review kit every 6 months; rotate perishable items; include cash and document copies

front: Red Flag: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What safety concern must you remember?
back: In pediatric MCI, undertriage (assigning too-low category) has higher mortality impact than in adults; err on the side of higher acuity when uncertain

front: Red Flag: Decontamination Procedures - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Contaminated personal items handled without proper procedures can cause secondary exposure to staff and other patients

back: Pre-printed triage tags and downtime forms readily accessible
rationale: Standardized forms mirror essential EHR documentation elements | Numbered/barcoded for later EHR reconciliation | Practice paper documentation during drills to maintain competency

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?

front: Identify the serotonin syndrome triad and administer cyproheptadine as the specific serotonin antagonist while managing hyperthermia and autonomic dysfunction

rationale: Serotonin syndrome results from excessive serotonergic activity at 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors. Trea

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?

rationale: Pneumonic plague (Yersinia pestis) is transmitted person-to-person via respiratory droplets, making it the most dangerous form of plague from a public health perspective. Unlike bubonic plague (which requires flea bite transmission), pneumonic plague can spread through cough droplets within approximately 3 feet. clinical_pearl: Pneumonic plague transmits via respiratory droplets within 3 feet

front: Follow the correct PPE doffing sequence with respirator removed last and buddy assistance to minimize contamination transfer during HAZMAT operations
back: Remove outer gloves, remove chemical suit (rolling downward from shoulders without touching outer surface), remove boot covers, remove respirator/PAPR last, remove inner gloves last; perform hand hygiene between each step; all removal occurs in the warm zone with a buddy for assistance
rationale: Level C PPE doffing sequence follows a contamination-reducing protocol: (1) Enter designated doffing area (warm-cold zone interface); (2) Buddy system: always doff with a trained assistant who can identify contamination breaches; (3) STEP 1: Remove outer gloves (most contaminated item); dispose in designated waste; (4) STEP 2: Open/unzip chemical-resistant suit; roll suit downward from shoulders, turning it inside-out as it is removed; avoid touching outer surface; (5) STEP 3: Remove boot covers
clinical_pearl: Outer gloves first (most contaminated)

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: <20 mg/kg: minimal; 20-60: moderate; >60: severe/lethal

front: Address chronic medication interruption as a medical emergency in disaster shelter populations
back: Obtain blood glucose check immediately, initiate insulin therapy through emergency medication provisions, and arrange for chronic medication replacement through pharmacy assistance programs or FEMA Individual Assistance
rationale: Chronic medication interruption is one of the most common and most dangerous health consequences of natural disasters. This diabetic patient without insulin for 2 days is at high risk for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) or hyperglycemic crisis, which can be fatal. Immediate priorities include: (1) Point-of-care blood glucose check; (2) Emergency insulin administration if hyperglycemic; (3) Assessment for signs of DKA (fruity breath, Kussmaul respirations, altered mental status, abdominal pain); (4)
clinical_pearl: Check blood glucose immediately in displaced diabetics

front: Clinical decision: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Tourniquet application takes 15-30 seconds during triage
rationale: Active arterial hemorrhage = immediate RED classification | Modern practice permits hemorrhage control during START triage | SALT explicitly incorporates lifesaving interventions in triage
clinical_pearl: Hemorrhage control (tourniquet application) is a 15-30 second intervention that should not be delayed during triage for active arterial bleeding

front: Clinical decision: Anticholinergic Toxidrome - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: QRS >100 ms = significant sodium channel blockade
rationale: QRS >160 ms predicts seizures and ventricular dysrhythmias | NaHCO3 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus; goal QRS <120 ms | Physostigmine contraindicated with QRS widening
clinical_pearl: Physostigmine is CONTRAINDICATED when QRS is widened; it can precipitate asystole. Use sodium bicarbonate for sodium channel blockade

front: Clinical decision: Heavy Metal Poisoning - What is the key clinical pearl?

front: Diagnose chronic lead poisoning by clinical presentation and venous blood lead level, and initiate chelation therapy based on level severity

rationale: Chronic lead poisoning (plumbism) presents with the classic triad: abdominal colic (lead colic), peripheral neuropathy (wrist drop from radial nerve motor neuropathy), and a bluish-gray line on the gingival margin (Burton's line from lead sulfide deposits). Additional findings include: constipation, fatigue, cognitive impairment, anemia (basophilic stippling on smear), nephropathy, and in children, developmental delays. Diagnosis: venous blood lead level (BLL) is the gold standard. BLL >5 μg/dL
clinical_pearl: Classic triad: abdominal colic, wrist drop, Burton's line

front: Red Flag: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning - What safety concern must you remember?

rationale: Carbon monoxide poisoning in pregnancy represents a special circumstance requiring aggressive treatment due to the unique vulnerability of the fetus. Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) has a higher affinity for CO than adult hemoglobin, resulting in fetal COHb levels approximately 10-15% higher than maternal levels with a longer elimination half-life. This means the fetus is exposed to significantly higher CO levels for longer periods than the mother. Additionally, the fetal oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve
clinical_pearl: Fetal hemoglobin has higher CO affinity than adult hemoglobin

front: Administer and monitor high-dose insulin euglycemic therapy for CCB overdose including frequent glucose and potassium monitoring with concurrent dextrose infusion

rationale: High-dose insulin euglycemic therapy (HIET) is a cornerstone of severe CCB overdose management: Mechanism: CCBs cause myocardial stunning partly by impairing myocardial glucose uptake. Insulin at high doses (far exceeding normal therapeutic doses) promotes myocardial glucose utilization through insulin-mediated glucose transporter (GLUT4) activation, improving cardiac contractility independent of calcium channel function. Protocol: (1) Insulin bolus: 1 unit/kg IV (regular insulin); (2) Insulin i
clinical_pearl: Insulin improves myocardial glucose uptake via GLUT4

front: Red Flag: Opioid Overdose and Naloxone - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Healthcare workers have experienced overdose symptoms from dermal and inhalation exposure to carfentanil; enhanced PPE is mandatory

front: Identify the sympathomimetic toxidrome and differentiate it from anticholinergic syndrome by the presence of diaphoresis, and manage with benzodiazepines and cooling

rationale: The sympathomimetic toxidrome results from excess catecholamine stimulation through various mechanisms: (1) Cocaine: blocks reuptake of norepinephrine, dopamine, and serotonin; also sodium channel blockade (cardiotoxicity); (2) Amphetamines: increase release AND block reuptake of catecholamines; methamphetamine is more potent and longer-acting than amphetamine; (3) MDMA: primarily serotonergic but also sympathomimetic; (4) Synthetic cathinones: variable mechanisms including reuptake inhibition a
clinical_pearl: Cocaine, amphetamines, cathinones, MDMA, ephedrine

front: Red Flag: Disaster Triage Categories - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Always perform a thorough secondary survey on MCI patients, including GREEN-tagged patients; occult penetrating wounds are easily missed in chaotic triage

front: Manage transdermal fentanyl overdose with continuous naloxone infusion accounting for the prolonged subcutaneous drug depot
back: Continuous naloxone infusion due to fentanyl's prolonged duration of action from transdermal absorption
rationale: Transdermal fentanyl patches create a subcutaneous depot that continues to release fentanyl for 72+ hours even after patch removal. The half-life of fentanyl from transdermal absorption is 17-27 hours, far exceeding naloxone's duration of action of 30-90 minutes. This creates a significant risk for re-narcotization (recurrence of opioid toxicity) after the naloxone wears off. A continuous naloxone infusion is required, typically calculated as two-thirds of the effective bolus dose per hour. The
clinical_pearl: Transdermal fentanyl half-life is 17-27 hours vs naloxone's 30-90 minutes

front: Classify blast injury mechanisms and recognize primary blast injury patterns affecting air-filled organs

rationale: Primary blast injury results from the interaction of the blast overpressure wave with the body. The overpressure wave preferentially damages air-filled organs and structures due to the physics of impedance mismatch at air-fluid interfaces. The three organs most susceptible to primary blast injury are: (1) Ears - tympanic membrane rupture is the most common primary blast injury and serves as a biomarker of blast exposure. Rupture typically occurs at overpressures of 5-15 psi. (2) Lungs - blast lu
clinical_pearl: Primary blast: overpressure wave damages air-filled organs (ears, lungs, bowel)

front: Prioritize airway assessment in pediatric laundry pod ingestion due to risk of oropharyngeal edema and aspiration
back: Evaluate airway patency and assess for signs of aspiration or airway edema, as laundry pod contents can cause rapid oropharyngeal and laryngeal swelling
rationale: Laundry detergent pod ingestion in young children is an increasingly common and potentially life-threatening exposure. The concentrated liquid detergent in pods has a pH of 7-8 (mildly alkaline) but contains highly concentrated surfactants that can cause severe mucosal injury, aspiration pneumonitis, and airway compromise. The most critical initial assessment is airway patency because: (1) The concentrated surfactants can cause rapid oropharyngeal and laryngeal edema, potentially occluding the a
clinical_pearl: Concentrated surfactants cause rapid mucosal injury and airway edema

front: Red Flag: Opioid Overdose and Naloxone - What safety concern must you remember?

clinical_pearl: Alpha-Bravo-Charlie model: 12h on, 24h off, continuous coverage

front: Identify perfusion failure in START triage as indicated by absent radial pulse requiring RED classification

This indicates significant hypotension (SBP likely <60-80 mmHg). The absence of radial pulse is a perfusion failure criterion in START triage, automatically classifying the patient as RED regardless of subsequent mental status assessment.

clinical_pearl: CroFab: 4-6 vials initially, then 2 vials at 6, 12, and 18 hours for maintenance

clinical_pearl: Benzodiazepines are first-line for agitation in sympathomimetic toxicity

clinical_pearl: Atropine = muscarinic blockade; pralidoxime = cholinesterase reactivation (muscarinic + nicotinic)

clinical_pearl: Irradiation: not radioactive, no contamination risk

front: Recognize the clinical presentation of arsenic poisoning and differentiate it from other heavy metal toxicities
back: Arsenic
rationale: This presentation is classic for acute arsenic poisoning. The combination of severe GI symptoms (abdominal pain, profuse watery 'rice water' diarrhea), garlic-like odor on breath, pancytopenia, and QT prolongation is pathognomonic for arsenic toxicity. Arsenic disrupts cellular respiration by interfering with oxidative phosphorylation and also causes direct toxicity to the GI mucosa, bone marrow, and cardiac conduction system. Key clinical features of arsenic poisoning include: acute GI symptoms
clinical_pearl: Arsenic: garlic odor, rice-water diarrhea, QT prolongation, pancytopenia, Mees lines

front: Differentiate isopropyl alcohol poisoning from methanol/EG by the presence of ketosis WITHOUT metabolic acidosis
back: Supportive care with monitoring, as isopropyl alcohol does not cause anion gap metabolic acidosis like methanol and ethylene glycol; hemodialysis only if hemodynamically unstable
rationale: Isopropyl alcohol (rubbing alcohol) poisoning differs fundamentally from methanol and ethylene glycol poisoning in that it does NOT produce a toxic metabolite that causes metabolic acidosis. Isopropyl alcohol is metabolized to acetone (which causes ketonemia and ketonuria but NOT acidosis). This is the key differentiating feature: isopropyl alcohol causes an osmolar gap and elevated ketones but a NORMAL anion gap (no acidosis). This is in contrast to methanol (formic acid → anion gap acidosis) a
clinical_pearl: Isopropyl alcohol → acetone (non-toxic metabolite)

front: Clinical decision: Toxic Alcohol Poisoning - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Visual changes from formic acid damage to optic nerve are pathognomonic
rationale: Fomepizole: 15 mg/kg IV loading, then 10 mg/kg q12h | Alternative: IV ethanol to maintain BAL 100-150 mg/dL | Folate enhances formate metabolism; give leucovorin 50 mg IV q6h
clinical_pearl: Snowstorm vision is pathognomonic for methanol; formic acid specifically damages the optic nerve. Fomepizole blocks the metabolic pathway

front: Identify ESF-8 as the federal Public Health and Medical Services function led by HHS/ASPR that coordinates medical response resources during disasters
back: ESF-8 is the Public Health and Medical Services function under the National Response Framework, led by the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)/ASPR, coordinating federal public health and medical response to disasters
rationale: Emergency Support Function 8 (ESF-8) is one of 15 ESFs in the National Response Framework (NRF). ESF-8 - Public Health and Medical Services: (1) Lead agency: Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), specifically the Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response (ASPR); (2) Scope: coordinating federal public health and medical support for state, tribal, and local jurisdictions during disasters; (3) Capabilities include: assessment of public health and medical needs; health surveill
clinical_pearl: Lead: HHS/ASPR for public health and medical services

front: Red Flag: Psychological First Aid - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Forcing trauma processing in the acute phase can worsen outcomes; PFA provides stabilization and safety, not therapy
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Do not force processing of the traumatic event

front: Use the time-to-emesis correlation to estimate radiation dose severity and predict prognosis in acute radiation exposure
back: The patient received a very high radiation dose (likely above 6 Gy), indicating severe acute radiation syndrome with poor prognosis
rationale: The timing of onset of prodromal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) after radiation exposure is the most important initial indicator of radiation dose and prognosis. This relationship is known as the 'Andrews Lymphocyte Depletion Kinetics' and the 'Time to Emesis' correlation. Onset of vomiting within 1 hour of exposure indicates a dose likely exceeding 6 Gy (Gray), which is associated with severe Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS) and a very poor prognosis without aggressive medical intervention
clinical_pearl: Time to emesis: <1h = >6 Gy, 1-2h = 4-6 Gy, 2-6h = 2-4 Gy, >6h = 1-2 Gy

front: Clinical decision: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: NMS tetrad: hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, AMS
rationale: Dantrolene: blocks calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum | Bromocriptine: dopamine agonist to restore blocked dopaminergic activity | CPK elevation confirms rhabdomyolysis from severe rigidity
clinical_pearl: NMS treatment: dantrolene for rigidity + bromocriptine for dopamine agonism; serotonin syndrome treatment: cyproheptadine - do NOT confuse these

front: Clinical decision: Pandemic Response Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Portable HEPA provides equivalent air filtration
rationale: Temporary negative pressure generators for standard rooms | Cohort confirmed positive patients together | Verify modifications with smoke testing or differential pressure
clinical_pearl: Portable HEPA units + negative pressure generators convert standard rooms to functional AII; cohort confirmed patients to free AII rooms for suspects

front: Establish an ICS Staging Area with a Staging Officer to manage ambulance flow during mass casualty incidents
back: Establish a Staging Area with a Staging Officer to control the flow of ambulances and ensure organized patient delivery to the triage area
rationale: The Staging Area is a critical ICS component for managing resource flow during MCIs. A Staging Officer is designated to: (1) Control ambulance arrival by directing them to a specific staging location; (2) Ensure organized unloading sequence, prioritizing patients based on field triage categories; (3) Communicate with the Triage Officer about incoming patient volume and acuity; (4) Prevent ambulance congestion at the ED entrance that blocks access; (5) Track ambulance availability for potential s
clinical_pearl: Staging Officer controls ambulance arrival and unloading sequence

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: 96-hour self-sufficiency supplies
rationale: Generator testing and fuel topping | Ride-out team pre-positioning before roads close | Move critical equipment above potential flood level
clinical_pearl: Shelter vs. evacuate decision by 48-72h before landfall; 96-hour self-sufficiency; ride-out team pre-positioning; move equipment above flood level

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Osmolar gap >10 suggests unmeasured osmoles from toxic alcohols
rationale: Early: high osmolar gap; Late: high anion gap as metabolism proceeds | Account for measured ethanol when calculating osmolar gap | Both gaps may normalize in late presentations creating a diagnostic challenge
clinical_pearl: The osmolar gap decreases as toxic alcohols are metabolized while the anion gap increases; both may normalize in late presentation

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: H2S inhibits cytochrome c oxidase similar to cyanide
rationale: Rotten egg smell at low concentrations but olfactory fatigue at high levels | Nitrites work by forming methemoglobin which binds sulfide ions | Hydroxocobalamin has emerging evidence for H2S poisoning
clinical_pearl: H2S causes olfactory fatigue at high concentrations, so absence of smell does not rule out exposure; mechanism is similar to cyanide poisoning

front: Describe medical intelligence as health-focused threat analysis that informs disaster preparedness planning, HVA development, and medical countermeasure stockpiling decisions
back: Medical intelligence is the collection and analysis of health-related data about threats, vulnerabilities, and consequences specific to healthcare delivery, including disease patterns, healthcare infrastructure status, pharmaceutical supply chains, and biological threat assessments; it differs from threat intelligence by focusing specifically on health impact and medical countermeasure readiness
rationale: Medical intelligence (MEDINT) in disaster preparedness: (1) Definition: systematic collection, evaluation, and analysis of health-related information about geographic areas, populations, and potential threats to support medical planning and decision-making; (2) Components: disease surveillance data (endemic diseases, emerging threats), healthcare infrastructure assessment (hospital capacity, supply chain status), environmental health data (water quality, air quality), biological and chemical thr
clinical_pearl: Disease surveillance, infrastructure assessment, threat analysis

front: Expand airborne infection isolation capacity using portable HEPA filtration, temporary negative pressure, cohorting, and alternative care sites during pandemic surge
back: Convert standard rooms to temporary negative pressure using portable HEPA filtration units and negative-pressure generators; cohort confirmed patients together; establish dedicated COVID/respiratory units; use anteroom barriers with HEPA-filtered air exhausts
rationale: AII surge strategies when negative pressure rooms are exhausted: (1) Portable HEPA filtration: standalone HEPA units in standard patient rooms provide equivalent air changes; position exhaust to direct airflow away from the door and toward the HEPA unit; (2) Temporary negative pressure: portable negative-pressure generators create differential pressure in standard rooms by exhausting filtered air to the outside; (3) Cohorting: confirmed positive patients can share rooms, freeing AII rooms for su
clinical_pearl: Portable HEPA provides equivalent air filtration

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Respiratory failure from scorpion envenomation in children can develop rapidly; have intubation equipment immediately available
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Airway protection essential due to salivation and aspiration risk

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Pyridoxine gram-for-gram matching INH dose; 5g empirically
rationale: INH triad: seizures, metabolic acidosis, coma | Benzodiazepines adjunctive but insufficient alone | Phenytoin is ineffective for INH seizures
clinical_pearl: INH seizures require pyridoxine (B6) because INH depletes B6 needed for GABA synthesis; standard anticonvulsants alone are insufficient

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: GREEN: no structural hazard found, normal operations
rationale: YELLOW: restricted areas, reduced occupancy, some operations continue | RED: extreme hazard, immediate evacuation, do NOT enter | Aftershocks can collapse damaged buildings
clinical_pearl: ATC-20: GREEN = safe to occupy, YELLOW = restricted use with limitations, RED = unsafe/evacuate. A standing building is NOT necessarily safe after earthquake

front: Perform dynamic re-triage when MCI patient condition changes, recognizing that triage categories are not permanent and continuous reassessment is essential
back: Immediately re-triage the patient to RED (Immediate) and move them to the RED treatment area for priority intervention; MCI triage is dynamic and patients must be continuously reassessed for category changes
rationale: Dynamic re-triage is a critical concept in MCI management. Initial triage categories are not permanent; they represent the patient's condition at the moment of assessment. Clinical deterioration requires immediate re-triage: (1) YELLOW → RED: if patient develops respiratory failure, hemodynamic instability, decreased consciousness, or other signs of imminent life threat; (2) YELLOW → GREEN: if patient's condition improves or initial assessment was too aggressive; (3) RED → BLACK: in true mass ca
clinical_pearl: Triage categories are not permanent assignments

front: Clinical decision: Serotonin Syndrome - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Hyperthermia triggers DIC at >104°F
rationale: SIADH + excessive water intake = hyponatremia | Antipyretics ineffective; use physical cooling methods | Young women at higher risk for severe hyponatremia
clinical_pearl: MDMA hyperthermia is from muscle hyperactivity not altered set point - antipyretics are ineffective; aggressive physical cooling is required

front: Recognize the synergistic CYP2C9 inhibition and gut flora suppression mechanisms by which TMP-SMX potentiates warfarin anticoagulation
back: TMP-SMX inhibits CYP2C9 (the primary enzyme metabolizing warfarin's S-enantiomer) and also has intrinsic anticoagulant effect by suppressing vitamin K-producing gut flora, synergistically potentiating warfarin
rationale: The TMP-SMX and warfarin interaction is one of the most clinically significant drug interactions in emergency medicine. Two mechanisms: (1) Pharmacokinetic: TMP-SMX inhibits CYP2C9, which metabolizes the more potent S-enantiomer of warfarin. This results in decreased warfarin clearance and increased plasma levels, particularly of the active S-warfarin; (2) Pharmacodynamic: the sulfamethoxazole component suppresses vitamin K-producing gut bacteria, reducing vitamin K availability for coagulation
clinical_pearl: CYP2C9 inhibition increases S-warfarin levels

front: Prioritize blast injury treatment over radiation concerns in dirty bomb casualties, recognizing that conventional explosive injuries dominate the acute phase
back: Primarily blast injuries and penetrating trauma from conventional explosives, with radiation contamination as a secondary concern; the explosion kills more people than the radiation in the acute phase
rationale: A 'dirty bomb' (radiological dispersal device or RDD) combines conventional explosives with radioactive material. In the acute phase, the primary injuries are from the explosion itself: (1) Primary blast injuries: blast lung, tympanic membrane rupture, bowel perforation; (2) Secondary blast injuries: penetrating trauma from shrapnel and debris; (3) Tertiary blast injuries: blunt trauma from being thrown; (4) Quaternary blast injuries: burns, crush injury, inhalation. The radiation component of a
clinical_pearl: Primary injuries from conventional explosives, not radiation

front: Clinical decision: Surge Capacity Management - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Clean delivery area with privacy from other shelter occupants
rationale: Delivery supplies: cord clamp, scissors, bulb syringe, clean towels | 34-week infant: preterm, may need respiratory support, keep warm | Continue arranging transport for mother and newborn to NICU facility
clinical_pearl: 34-week premature infant needs warmth, respiratory monitoring, and NICU transfer; field delivery supplies include cord clamp, scissors, bulb syringe, and clean towels

front: Clinical decision: Toxic Ingestion in Pediatrics - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Esophageal battery causes alkaline burn via electrolysis within 2 hours
rationale: AP and lateral X-ray to locate and confirm it is a button battery | Complications: perforation, fistula, fatal hemorrhage | Activated charcoal is contraindicated
clinical_pearl: Esophageal button battery causes full-thickness burn within 2 hours; removal is an emergency regardless of symptoms

front: Recognize pulse oximetry limitations in methemoglobinemia and use co-oximetry for accurate assessment when SpO2 reading plateaus around 85% in well-appearing patients
back: Pulse oximetry is unreliable in methemoglobinemia because methemoglobin absorbs light at both wavelengths used by the oximeter, causing falsely low readings that plateau around 85% regardless of true oxygen saturation; co-oximetry is needed for accurate measurement
rationale: Methemoglobinemia creates a unique pulse oximetry artifact. Standard pulse oximeters use two wavelengths of light (660 nm red, 940 nm infrared) and calculate oxygen saturation based on the ratio of absorbance. Oxyhemoglobin absorbs more infrared; deoxyhemoglobin absorbs more red. Methemoglobin absorbs both wavelengths equally, causing the oximeter to calculate a ratio close to 1:1, which corresponds to approximately 85% SpO2 on the calibration curve. As a result, SpO2 tends toward 85% regardless
clinical_pearl: MetHb absorbs both wavelengths equally → ratio ~1:1 → SpO2 ~85%

front: Clinical decision: Incident Command System (ICS) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Single source of information prevents conflicting statements
rationale: Media area away from treatment and reunification areas | HIPAA: aggregate data only, no patient-identifiable information | Crisis communication: be first, be right, be credible
clinical_pearl: PIO = single source of all public communication; press briefings every 30-60 min; HIPAA compliance; no patient-identifiable information without consent

front: Red Flag: Opioid Overdose and Naloxone - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Always secure the airway first in patients with severe respiratory depression regardless of anticipated naloxone response
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Naloxone duration of action (30-90 min) is shorter than most opioids, requiring monitoring for re-narcotization

front: Clinical decision: Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear (CBRN) Response - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Ricin: ribosomal inactivation → protein synthesis cessation → cell death
rationale: No antidote or antitoxin available | Inhalation exposure causes progressive ARDS, often fatal within 36-72 hours | Standard and droplet precautions sufficient; ricin not easily aerosolized from patients
clinical_pearl: There is NO antidote for ricin; management is purely supportive including ventilation, vasopressors, and organ support

front: Clinical decision: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: First START triage question: Can the patient walk?
rationale: Ambulatory = GREEN regardless of injuries | GREEN patients need periodic re-triage as they can deteriorate | Direct walking wounded to a separate treatment area
clinical_pearl: In START triage, ability to walk automatically classifies a patient as GREEN regardless of visible injuries; do not over-triage ambulatory patients

front: Red Flag: Anticholinergic Toxidrome - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Anticholinergic burden in elderly patients increases fall risk, cognitive decline, and delirium; advocate for medication reconciliation
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Elderly patients are most susceptible to anticholinergic burden

front: Red Flag: Surge Capacity Management - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Surge ICU without adequate staff training and monitoring equipment produces worse outcomes than appropriate crisis triage and allocation
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Systems: documentation, communication, patient tracking, ICS

front: Red Flag: Surge Capacity Management - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Modified staffing ratios during crisis surge require careful triage to ensure the sickest patients receive adequate nursing attention
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Expedite admissions and fast-track discharges for stable patients

front: Clinical decision: Psychological First Aid - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Disaster behavioral phases: heroic, honeymoon, disillusionment, reconstruction
rationale: Disillusionment: conflicts, hoarding, sleep problems, substance abuse | Accelerated by shelter crowding, loss of privacy, uncertainty | Continued PFA, resource connection, conflict mediation needed
clinical_pearl: The disillusionment phase often catches helpers off-guard as initial community solidarity transitions to conflict and despair; continued support is essential

front: Red Flag: Blast Injury Patterns - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Untreated tension pneumothorax causes rapid cardiovascular collapse and death; needle decompression is a life-saving intervention that takes seconds
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Follow with chest tube as definitive management

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Ventilator patients: immediate manual BVM ventilation needed
rationale: IV pumps: transition to gravity drip calculations | Medication refrigeration: insulin and blood products at risk | Simultaneously implement manual backups and arrange power restoration
clinical_pearl: Generator failure = life support failure; each ventilator patient needs a dedicated person for manual ventilation, and IV pumps need gravity drip calculations

front: Red Flag: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Medical intelligence gaps lead to inadequate preparedness for predictable threats; continuous monitoring and analysis are essential for proactive planning
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Nurses contribute through disease reporting and surveillance

front: Clinical decision: Organophosphate Exposure - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: IMS: proximal weakness, neck flexors, respiratory muscles, cranial nerves
rationale: Occurs 24-96 hours after initial crisis resolution | Does not respond to atropine or pralidoxime | Recovery in 5-18 days as new acetylcholinesterase is synthesized
clinical_pearl: Intermediate syndrome occurs after cholinergic crisis resolves; it does NOT respond to atropine or pralidoxime - supportive care with ventilation is the treatment

front: Recognize that children are at higher risk for CO toxicity at lower COHb levels due to higher metabolic demands and developing nervous system
back: The 4-year-old child, because children have higher metabolic rates, higher oxygen demands, and are more susceptible to CO toxicity at lower levels
rationale: While the 4-year-old has the lowest COHb level at 8%, children are at significantly higher risk for CO toxicity at lower levels due to several factors: (1) Higher metabolic rate and oxygen consumption per kilogram means tissue hypoxia develops more rapidly; (2) Higher minute ventilation relative to body size results in faster CO accumulation if re-exposed; (3) Developing nervous system is more susceptible to hypoxic-ischemic injury; (4) Fetal hemoglobin (if residual HbF is still present in very
clinical_pearl: Children have higher metabolic rates and oxygen demands making them more susceptible

front: Escalate benzodiazepine therapy for worsening alcohol withdrawal based on CIWA-Ar trending and prepare for ICU-level care
back: Increase benzodiazepine dosing frequency and dose per the symptom-triggered protocol; prepare for potential ICU transfer for DTs
rationale: The escalating CIWA-Ar score from 12 to 25 with new neuropsychiatric symptoms indicates progression toward severe alcohol withdrawal and impending delirium tremens. CIWA-Ar score interpretation: 0-9 mild (may not require pharmacotherapy), 10-18 moderate (symptom-triggered dosing indicated), 19+ severe (aggressive treatment required). The increase from 12 to 25 represents clinical deterioration requiring dose escalation. Symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing should be intensified: increase both
clinical_pearl: CIWA-Ar: 0-9 mild, 10-18 moderate, 19+ severe requiring aggressive treatment

front: Clinical decision: Alcohol Withdrawal and DTs - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: CIWA-Ar: 0-9 mild, 10-18 moderate, 19+ severe requiring aggressive treatment
rationale: Symptom-triggered dosing: increase dose and frequency for rising CIWA-Ar | Phenytoin is ineffective for alcohol withdrawal seizures | Consider phenobarbital for benzodiazepine-resistant withdrawal
clinical_pearl: Phenytoin does NOT prevent alcohol withdrawal seizures; benzodiazepines are the mainstay. CIWA-Ar trending is more important than any single score

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Higher concentration surfactants + alkaline pH
rationale: Aspiration pneumonitis from surfactant pulmonary injury | CNS depression in children from pods | No ipecac or activated charcoal for caustic substances
clinical_pearl: Laundry pods are more toxic than regular detergent; aspiration of surfactant causes chemical pneumonitis; do NOT induce vomiting (caustic substance)

front: Red Flag: Acetaminophen Overdose and NAC Protocol - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Calculation errors are common with the three-phase NAC protocol; use standardized order sets or calculators to prevent dosing mistakes

clinical_pearl: Cap weight at 100 kg to prevent fluid overload

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Eastern diamondback envenomation can cause fatal hemorrhagic shock and coagulopathy; do not delay transfer for definitive antivenom therapy

clinical_pearl: Eastern diamondback: most dangerous North American pit viper

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: HIET requires aggressive glucose and potassium monitoring; iatrogenic hypoglycemia and hypokalemia are the primary risks

clinical_pearl: Transcutaneous pacing often fails in severe CCB toxicity

front: Clinical decision: Decontamination Procedures - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Intercept contaminated walk-ins BEFORE they enter the ED
rationale: Clothing removal = 80% of decontamination | Don appropriate PPE before contact with contaminated patient | Identify the chemical through employer contact or SDS sheets
clinical_pearl: Self-referred contaminated patients are MORE dangerous than EMS-transported patients because they bypass field decontamination and arrive unannounced

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Insulin glargine duration of action up to 24 hours
rationale: Continuous D10W infusion needed, not just dextrose boluses | Monitor glucose every 1-2 hours for at least 24 hours | Glucagon becomes ineffective once glycogen stores are depleted
clinical_pearl: Long-acting insulin (glargine) requires 24+ hours of glucose monitoring and dextrose infusion; a single bolus correction is insufficient

front: Red Flag: Serotonin Syndrome - What safety concern must you remember?
back: MDMA-induced hyperthermia above 106°F has near 100% mortality without aggressive cooling; begin cooling immediately without delay

clinical_pearl: Young women at higher risk for severe hyponatremia

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Half-life 20-130 days vs warfarin 20-60 hours
rationale: ~100x more potent than pharmaceutical warfarin | Vitamin K1 25-50 mg PO BID-TID for weeks to months | Contaminated synthetic cannabinoids are a reported source

front: Red Flag: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Understanding both kinetics and dynamics is essential for predicting symptom onset timing and selecting the appropriate treatment window
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: NAC timing (kinetics) and glutathione replenishment (dynamics)

front: Clinical decision: Decontamination Procedures - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Clothing removal eliminates 80-90% of external radioactive contamination
rationale: Lukewarm water: not hot (opens pores) or cold (traps particles) | Do not abrade intact skin; it drives contamination deeper | Scan with radiation detector after each wash cycle
clinical_pearl: Vigorous scrubbing drives radioactive particles deeper into skin; gentle washing with lukewarm water and mild soap is the correct technique

front: Clinical decision: Acetaminophen Overdose and NAC Protocol - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: RSTD depletes glutathione without recovery between doses
rationale: Nomogram is not applicable for chronic/RSTD toxicity | Parents may dose correctly each time but too frequently | Multiple APAP-containing products cause unintentional overdose
clinical_pearl: RSTD is different from acute ingestion; the nomogram cannot be used, and clinical evidence of hepatotoxicity guides NAC initiation

front: Clinical decision: Toxic Alcohol Poisoning - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Isopropyl alcohol → acetone (non-toxic metabolite)
rationale: Ketosis WITHOUT metabolic acidosis (normal pH and bicarb) | No need for fomepizole or ethanol since metabolite is non-toxic | Hemodialysis only for hemodynamic instability

front: Red Flag: Toxic Ingestion in Pediatrics - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Button battery in the esophagus is a true surgical emergency; never delay removal for observation or decontamination attempts
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Honey may be given while awaiting endoscopy for children over 12 months

front: Identify and stockpile essential medication categories for disaster pharmaceutical preparedness across trauma, antidote, and chronic disease needs
back: Antibiotics, analgesics, sedatives, vasopressors, antidotes (atropine, pralidoxime, cyanide kit), blood products, and chronic disease medications (insulin, antihypertensives, seizure medications)
rationale: A comprehensive disaster pharmaceutical cache must address multiple categories of medication needs: (1) Antibiotics: broad-spectrum for infection, plus specific agents for biological threats (doxycycline, ciprofloxacin for anthrax/plague); (2) Analgesics: opioids and non-opioids for mass trauma pain management; (3) Sedatives/anxiolytics: benzodiazepines for procedural sedation, seizure control, and agitation; (4) Vasopressors and resuscitation drugs: epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine for sho

front: Red Flag: Disaster Triage Categories - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Disaster situations create opportunities for child trafficking and custody disputes; verify identity before releasing any pediatric patient
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: Identity verification required before child release

front: Apply the tiered emergency blood release protocol transitioning from uncrossmatched O-negative to type-specific blood to conserve universal donor supply during MCI
back: Tier 1: immediate release of O-negative RBCs without crossmatch (2-5 minutes); Tier 2: type-specific but uncrossmatched blood once ABO type is confirmed (15-20 minutes); Tier 3: fully crossmatched blood (45-60 minutes); transition from Tier 1 to Tier 2 ASAP to conserve O-negative supply
rationale: Emergency blood release follows a tiered approach balancing speed with safety: (1) TIER 1 - Emergency release (2-5 minutes): O-negative RBCs released without type or crossmatch; used for hemorrhagic shock patients whose blood type is unknown; O-negative is the universal donor RBC type; limited supply must be conserved; (2) TIER 2 - Type-specific uncrossmatched (15-20 minutes): once ABO and Rh typing is completed, type-specific RBCs are released without full crossmatch; 99.8% compatible; minor an
clinical_pearl: Tier 1: O-negative, no crossmatch, 2-5 minutes

front: Clinical decision: Hospital Emergency Preparedness - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Tabletop exercises identify EOP gaps in low-stress environment
rationale: Pediatric surge is a known vulnerability for non-pediatric hospitals | Gaps require formal improvement planning with timeline and responsible parties | Validate improvement plans through future exercises
clinical_pearl: All hospitals, not just children's hospitals, must plan for pediatric surge; tabletop exercises are designed to identify these gaps in a safe environment

front: Clinical decision: Incident Command System (ICS) - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Staging Officer controls ambulance arrival and unloading sequence
rationale: Separate staging from triage and treatment areas | Clear traffic flow patterns for incoming and outgoing vehicles | Communicate capacity and patient flow with Triage Officer
clinical_pearl: The Staging Area and Officer control resource flow; without staging, ambulance congestion blocks access and disrupts the entire response

front: Red Flag: Serotonin Syndrome - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Serotonin syndrome can rapidly progress to multi-organ failure; early recognition and treatment are essential
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: Treatment includes cyproheptadine, benzodiazepines, and cooling

front: Red Flag: Heavy Metal Poisoning - What safety concern must you remember?
back: Chronic arsenic exposure significantly increases cancer risk (skin, lung, bladder, liver); all confirmed arsenic exposure patients need long-term cancer screening
rationale: From: Toxicology
clinical_pearl: 24-hour urine arsenic >50 mcg/L confirms diagnosis

front: Define LD50, ED50, TD50, and therapeutic index and explain their clinical relevance for medication safety and therapeutic drug monitoring
back: LD50 is the dose lethal to 50% of a test population; therapeutic index is the ratio of TD50 (toxic dose for 50%) to ED50 (effective dose for 50%), indicating the safety margin of a drug
rationale: Key toxicology concepts: (1) LD50 (Lethal Dose 50): the dose of a substance that kills 50% of a test population under standardized conditions. Lower LD50 = more toxic substance. Used primarily in preclinical/animal studies; (2) ED50 (Effective Dose 50): the dose that produces the desired therapeutic effect in 50% of the population; (3) TD50 (Toxic Dose 50): the dose that produces toxic effects in 50% of the population; (4) Therapeutic Index (TI): TI = TD50/ED50. A higher TI means a larger margin
clinical_pearl: LD50: dose lethal to 50% of test population

front: Red Flag: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI) Triage (START/JumpSTART) - What safety concern must you remember?
back: This is one of the most psychologically challenging aspects of MCI triage; nurses must be prepared through training and psychological support
rationale: From: Disaster Medicine
clinical_pearl: JumpSTART adds rescue breaths step before BLACK in children

front: Clinical decision: Toxicology Screening and Antidote Protocols - What is the key clinical pearl?
back: Liquefactive necrosis starts within 15 minutes
rationale: Perforation possible within 2 hours | Aortoesophageal fistula is a fatal complication | Do not wait for NPO status for removal

front: Red Flag: Toxic Alcohol Poisoning - What safety concern must you remember?

rationale: From: Toxicology
